title: ListHub: Save/favorite lists you like
description: Add ability for users to save/bookmark/favorite lists created by others. Needs a new table (e.g. saved_item with user_id + item_id) and UI to view saved lists. Like a 'star' or 'save' button on public lists.
close_reason: Deferred to WikiHub (star system built there)

title: ListHub: normalize quote formatting conventions for append API
description:
When appending quotes to lists (especially quoteslist), the formatting should follow existing conventions in the list. Current issues observed:
- Attribution put on separate line with ― prefix instead of inline ~Name pattern used elsewhere in the list
- No normalization of quote formatting on append

Proposed norms (based on existing quoteslist patterns):
- Use ~Name for attribution (e.g. ~Pierre Teilhard De Chardin), not ― Name on a new line
- Quote text on its own line(s), attribution on same or next line with ~
- Blank line separator before new quotes
- Consider building a quote-aware formatter that detects quote+attribution patterns and normalizes them

Need to audit existing quoteslist to confirm the dominant pattern and codify it. This is part of a broader need: the append API should be content-type-aware (quotes list vs. plain list vs. wiki page) and format accordingly.

title: ListHub: Agent self-service onboarding (signup, API key, MCP/skill)
description:
Make it trivial for AI agents to onboard themselves to ListHub without human intervention. Three parts:

1. **Agent self-registration**: API endpoint for agents to create accounts programmatically (POST /api/v1/auth/register). No CAPTCHA/email verification for agent accounts — just username + optional display name. Return credentials immediately.

2. **Agent API key provisioning**: After registration (or for existing accounts), agents should be able to create their own API keys via the API without needing a browser session. Currently key management requires @login_required (session only) — need to support Bearer token auth for key creation too.

3. **Agent discovery layer**: Make ListHub's capabilities obvious to agents:
   - llms.txt at root describing the API and how to self-onboard
   - MCP server (so Claude Code, Cursor, etc. can natively connect)
   - Claude Code skill (SKILL.md) for quick integration
   - OpenAPI spec / agents.json
   - The /api/docs page should have a machine-readable section, not just HTML

4. **Documentation**: The agent onboarding flow should be THE first thing in the API docs. Not buried — front and center. 'Here is how your agent gets started in 3 API calls.'

DISCUSS BEFORE IMPLEMENTING — this has security implications (abuse, spam accounts, rate limiting). Need to decide: open registration vs invite codes, rate limits, account tiers, etc.

title: ListHub vision: publish entire personal memory with selective public/private
description:
## Vision
ListHub should eventually become the *publishing layer* for Jacob's whole personal memory (`~/memory/` and other private knowledge stores), with fine-grained visibility control so each note/file/topic can independently be public, unlisted, shared, or private.

## Why
Right now there are two distinct pools of knowledge that don't talk to each other:
1. Private research in `~/memory/research/<area>/<topic>.md` — full PII, raw quotes, source attribution
2. Curated public lists in ListHub — sanitized, hashtagged, sharable

Today they have to be maintained as paired files by hand (see sibling brainstorm ticket on the research-bundle convention). The vision is that ListHub becomes the layer that handles this automatically:

- Point ListHub at a directory (e.g. `~/memory/research/`)
- Each markdown file becomes an item, defaulting to private
- Front-matter on each file declares visibility + sanitization rules
- A `public_view:` field can point at a sanitized derivative (or ListHub auto-generates one with PII redacted)
- Edits in either place sync both ways

## Adjacent capabilities this would enable
- **Cross-referencing** between private masters and their public faces (graph-style)
- **PII detection** at publish-time (warn if a phone number, full name, or address is in a public-tagged file)
- **Topic dashboards** showing "of my N research items in this area, X are public, Y are private"
- **Memory-to-ListHub sync** as a daemon (similar to how `bd sync` works for beads)

## Inspiration
This is conceptually adjacent to:
- Obsidian Publish (but with per-note visibility)
- Quartz / digital garden tools
- The Noos vision of layered knowledge (private daily logs → curated briefing → shared graph)

## First milestone
Spec out the file-system → ListHub sync model. Design the front-matter schema. Build a one-shot import script that walks `~/memory/research/` and creates ListHub items for any file whose front-matter declares `listhub_visibility: public` (or similar).

## Related
- Companion brainstorm ticket on the research-bundle convention (paired private/public files) — that's the manual version of this
- Existing tickets: code-6gy (visibility badges), code-umo (unlisted visibility), code-gm6 (per-user sharing)

title: ListHub: Real-time collaborative editing
description: Add real-time collaborative editing for shared items (like Google Docs). Would need WebSocket/SSE layer + CRDT or OT for conflict resolution. This is a stretch goal that builds on per-user write sharing (code-xxx). Consider: markdown-based OT is simpler than rich text, could use Yjs. Low priority since the git-based workflow already handles async collaboration.

title: ListHub: Make lists publicly editable
description: Add ability for lists to be publicly editable (anyone can add/edit items). Currently items are only editable by owner. Could use a new visibility level like 'public-edit' or a separate 'editable' flag on the item/share table.

title: ListHub: Show visibility badges on items in explore/directory/profile
description: Display the visibility status (public, unlisted, shared, private) as a badge/icon on each item wherever items are listed: explore page, user profiles, dashboard, search results. Currently there's no visual indicator of an item's sharing status. Users should be able to see at a glance what's public vs shared vs unlisted. Also show who an item is shared with (e.g., '3 people') on the item detail page.
close_reason: SVG visibility badges shown on all listhub surfaces

title: ListHub: Public git repo directory at /git/
description: Consider adding a public listing of all user git repos at listhub.globalbr.ai/git/. Currently repos are accessible via git clone but not discoverable. Questions to consider: (1) Should all public repos be listed or opt-in? (2) Privacy implications — repo names reveal usernames and content slugs. (3) Could be a nice discovery mechanism. (4) Security: make sure only public items' repos are browsable, not private ones.

title: ListHub: Keep API docs and GUI in sync for sharing/visibility features
description: CLAUDE.md already says 'When you add, modify, or remove any API endpoint, you MUST update the API docs template to match.' This ticket is a reminder: as unlisted visibility (code-umo), per-user write sharing (code-gm6), and public editing (code-6fl) are implemented, the API docs at /api/docs AND the web UI must stay in sync. Specifically: (1) API docs must document all visibility values including 'unlisted', (2) Share endpoint docs must show read vs write permission, (3) GUI share dialog must expose the same options as the API, (4) No feature should be API-only or GUI-only — both interfaces should have parity.
close_reason: Visibility badges + unlisted in API + perms at top of edit form

title: ListHub→WikiHub data migration: import 7K+ items as WikiHub pages
description: One-time migration of ListHub SQLite items into WikiHub. Each user's items become pages in their personal wiki. file_path subfolder structure (startx/lists/file.md) maps to WikiHub page paths. Visibility mapped to frontmatter. Timestamps/slugs preserved. Natural project folders can optionally split into project wikis post-import. Requires WikiHub deployed first.

title: ListHub: Auto-generate slug from list title
description: When creating a new list/item, auto-generate the slug from the title (slugify: lowercase, replace spaces with hyphens, strip special chars). Currently slug must be manually provided. Should be the default, with option to override.
close_reason: Auto-slug implemented in listhub edit form

title: ListHub: Table of contents + collapsible sections + expand-all toggle for long markdown items
description:
## Motivation Long, sectioned markdown lists on ListHub are currently hard to scan. The concrete trigger is the just-published [@jacobreal/depression-resources](https://listhub.globalbr.ai/@jacobreal/depression-resources) — a long resource list with many H2/H3 sections. A reader landing on it has no overview of the structure, no way to jump to a specific section, and no way to fold sections they're not currently interested in. The page is essentially a wall of text. This problem will get worse as ListHub becomes the publishing layer for personal memory (per code-5ss): research bundles, curriculum maps, resource indexes, and noos exports will all tend to be long and sectioned. The sister project `markdown-reader` (OpenMarkdownReader, the desktop app at `/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/`) already solves all three of these problems with a clean implementation we can mirror. This ticket is about porting that UX to ListHub's server-rendered Jinja2 templates. ## Current state (verified 2026-04-07) ListHub renders markdown server-side via Python's `markdown` library in `/Users/Jacob/listhub/views.py`: ```python def render_md(text): return markdown.markdown( text or '', extensions=['tables', 'fenced_code', 'toc', 'nl2br', 'sane_lists'] ) ``` Notable findings: - **The `toc` extension is already loaded** but never consumed. It auto-assigns `id="..."` attributes to every heading (so anchor links / `#section` jumps already work), but no template inserts a `[TOC]` marker or accesses `Markdown(...).toc` to actually display a table of contents. - **No collapsible sections.** The item view template (`/Users/Jacob/listhub/templates/item.html`) just dumps `{{ rendered_content|safe }}` into a `.content-body` div with zero JS post-processing. - **No expand-all / collapse-all.** N/A — there are no collapsible sections to control. - **Layout context:** `templates/base.html` already has an `app-layout` with a left sidebar (`_sidebar.html`) and a `
`. The item page has room on the right for a sticky ToC column on wide viewports. There is no JS framework — just static `static/style.css`, `static/sidebar.css`, `static/sidebar.js`. This is a good fit for a small vanilla-JS enhancement. So: **none of the three features exist on ListHub today.** Heading anchors do exist (free from the `toc` extension), which makes implementation easier. ## Desired behavior ### 1. Auto-generated table of contents - Builds from the `

`–`

` headings already present in `rendered_content`. - Indented by heading level (H2 < H3 < H4 visually nested). - Click a ToC entry → smooth-scroll to that heading. If the target heading is inside a collapsed section, auto-expand its ancestors first (mirror markdown-reader's `expandSectionAncestors`). - **Sticky** on wide viewports (≥1100px or so): pinned to the right of the content column, scrolls with the page. On narrow viewports: collapses to a single "Contents" disclosure at the top of the page (or a floating button), so it doesn't eat mobile screen real estate. - **Active-section highlight** as the user scrolls (use `IntersectionObserver`, not scroll events — see CSS-Tricks reference below). - Hidden entirely if the document has fewer than ~3 headings (don't clutter short notes). ### 2. Collapsible H2/H3 sections - Each heading gets a small chevron toggle (▸ / ▾) that expands/collapses the body of its section. - A "section" = the heading and everything until the next sibling heading of equal or lower level (standard outline semantics). - Click the chevron → toggle. Click the heading text itself → still does normal anchor-link behavior (so deep links keep working). - Default state: all expanded (the page reads top-to-bottom on first load). - Persist collapse state in `localStorage` keyed by the page slug, so a returning reader keeps their view. (Optional polish — see acceptance criteria.) - Empty sections (heading with no body) get a faded toggle and aren't interactive. ### 3. Expand-all / collapse-all - A single button-pair (or one toggle button) lives **inside the ToC panel header** ("Expand all" / "Collapse all"). - Clicking it expands or collapses every section on the page in one go. - If currently mixed/all-expanded → button shows "Collapse all". If all collapsed → "Expand all". (Or just a two-button row, simpler.) - Updates the persisted state. ## Reference implementation (markdown-reader) The markdown-reader project already has features 1 and 2 implemented in vanilla JS. File paths and key functions: - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/index.html` lines 76–80** — the ToC toggle button markup. - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/index.html` ~166+** — the `#toc-panel`, `#toc-content`, `#toc-close` markup that gets appended next to the main content. - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/renderer.js` lines 3585–3632** — `extractHeadings()`, `renderTOC()`, and `toggleTOC()`. These are ~50 lines total and almost directly copy-pastable. ToC items are rendered as: ```html ${text} ``` with click handlers that scroll to the target and call `expandSectionAncestors`. - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/renderer.js` lines 3862–3934** — `buildCollapsibleSections()`. This is the **key reusable algorithm**: it walks the rendered markdown body, groups headings + their following content into nested `
` wrappers with a `
` inside each. Stack-based, handles nested levels correctly, ~70 lines. **This is the function to port.** - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/renderer.js` line 3850** — the `collapseToggle` button injected into each heading: ``. (In ListHub we'd inject this client-side after render, since render is server-side.) - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/renderer.js` lines 3949–3963** — `expandSectionAncestors(target)`. Walks up `.md-section` parents and removes the `collapsed` class. Needed so deep links / ToC clicks always reveal the target. - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/renderer.js` lines 5481–5497** — the click delegation handler on `.collapse-toggle`. Single delegated listener on the markdown body, ~15 lines. - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/styles.css` lines 1580–1614** — the CSS for `.md-section`, `.md-section-body`, `.md-section.collapsed`, the rotating chevron, and faded empty-section toggles. - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/styles.css` lines 2142–2261** — `.toc-panel`, `.toc-header`, `.toc-content`, `.toc-item[data-level="N"]` indentation rules, and the `.toc-toggle-btn`. **What markdown-reader does NOT have yet:** expand-all / collapse-all. They have an open ticket for it (`markdown-reader-r12`). So this is genuinely new work for both projects, and ListHub will leapfrog markdown-reader on that one feature. ## Proposed approach **Server-side vs client-side: client-side, post-render JS enhancement.** Reasoning: 1. ListHub is server-rendered Jinja2 with no JS framework. Adding a small ~150-line vanilla JS file fits the existing style (`static/sidebar.js` is the precedent). 2. Heading IDs are already generated server-side by Python-markdown's `toc` extension, so anchors and deep links work without any backend change. 3. Building the ToC and collapsible wrappers client-side means **zero changes to `views.py` or `render_md()`** — we don't have to risk breaking the existing markdown pipeline. The HTML payload stays the same; we just decorate it after the DOM is parsed. 4. The same JS works for any future template that emits a `.content-body` div (item view, future preview, etc.). **Library choice: no library.** Reasons: - markdown-reader's implementation is ~150 lines of vanilla JS total. Porting it directly is faster than learning Tocbot's API. - A library like [Tocbot](https://tscanlin.github.io/tocbot/) (~3.6 KB) would handle the ToC half but not the collapsible-sections half, so we'd still need custom code for collapsibles. Not worth a dependency for just ToC. - Use `IntersectionObserver` for the active-state highlight (native API, well-supported, the recommended pattern per [CSS-Tricks](https://css-tricks.com/sticky-table-of-contents-with-scrolling-active-states/) and [Jonathan Lau](https://blog.jonathanlau.io/posts/create-interactive-toc-in-markdown-generated-content/)). **File layout:** ``` static/ toc.js ← new: extractHeadings, renderTOC, buildCollapsibleSections, expandSectionAncestors, expandAll, collapseAll, IntersectionObserver active-state, localStorage persistence toc.css ← new: .md-section / .md-section-body / .collapsed / .collapse-toggle / .toc-panel / .toc-item[data-level] / sticky positioning / mobile breakpoint templates/ item.html ← edit: add +

title: ListHub search: prioritize title/slug matches over content matches
description: When searching under My Lists, results should rank title/filename matches above content matches. Needs more design thought on the right UX — e.g. show title matches first then content matches, or weighted scoring.
close_reason: FTS5 bm25 ranking with title 3x, tags 2x, content 1x weights + match-type indicators

title: ListHub: Per-user sharing with write permission
description: The share table already supports per-user sharing (item_id, shared_with, permission) but permission is always 'read'. Implement 'write' permission so specific users can edit shared items. This is the foundation for the 'publicly editable' feature (code-6fl) — start with named-user write access, then potentially add 'anyone with link can edit' later. UI: share dialog on item page to add users + set read/write.

title: Research-bundle convention: paired private/public files for ListHub topics
description:
Brainstorm captured 2026-04-07 while organizing depression resources from a 48h iMessage thread.

## Goal
Have a clean way to keep PRIVATE research notes (with names, phone numbers, raw quotes, source attribution) AND publish a SANITIZED public version on ListHub, with the two clearly linked.

## Options considered
**A. Paired ListHub items, slug convention** — `<topic>` (public) + `<topic>-notes` (private). Pros: one tool. Cons: drifts; sources get duplicated as topic count grows.

**B. Personal memory for private + ListHub for public** ← CHOSEN FOR NOW
- Private master: `~/memory/research/<area>/<topic>.md` (full PII)
- Public publish: `jacobreal/<topic>` on ListHub (sanitized, hashtagged)
- Cross-link via front-matter (`public_listhub:` field) and footer ("Curated from private research — DM for sources")
- Same slug stem on both sides → grep-able
- Why: kind-of-data boundary, not just visibility boundary. Phone numbers belong in `~/memory/`, not on a server set up for note publishing.

**C. ListHub directory hierarchy** (`/topics/mental-health/depression/{public,private}`) — repurposing the directory feature for visibility tiers. Same drift problem as A.

**D. True graph in Noos** — each resource = node, each person = node, edges (`recommended_by`, `lineage`, `treats`, `located_in`). ListHub list becomes a *rendered view* of a graph query. Patricia Walden lives once and shows up wherever edges point. This is what Jacob actually described wanting ("knowledge graph with labeled edges"). Heavyweight — worth it once Jacob has done this exercise for 3+ topics and feels duplication.

## Cheap version of "labeled edges" in the meantime
Inside the private master file, use `[[wiki-links]]` to people and modalities:
- `**[[Patricia Walden]]** recommended by [[Jacob]] · lineage: [[Iyengar]] · location: [[Boston]]`
- When eventually moving to Noos, those `[[brackets]]` are 80% of the parsing work.

## Naming convention to lock in
- `~/memory/research/<area>/<topic>.md` = private master
- `jacobreal/<topic>` on ListHub = public version
- Front-matter on private file: `public_listhub: <url>`, `source_session: <claude session id>`
- Public ListHub item footer: "Curated from private research — DM for sources"

## When to revisit
After Jacob has built ≥3 research bundles and starts feeling the redundancy of rewriting cross-cutting people/modalities. At that point, evaluate promotion to Noos with a render-to-ListHub sync step.

title: ListHub: Add 'unlisted' visibility option
description: Add 'unlisted' as a 4th visibility level. Unlisted items are accessible via direct URL but don't appear in the directory, explore page, or user profile listings. Like YouTube unlisted videos. Current levels: private, shared, public. New: private < shared < unlisted < public. Unlisted items should still sync to git and be accessible via the API if you know the slug/ID.
close_reason: Unlisted added to VALID_VISIBILITIES in listhub API

title: ListHub: Dashboard visibility filter buttons (backend exists, UI doesn't)
description:
## Problem
There's no way in the ListHub UI to see at a glance which of your items are public vs private. To answer 'what have I published publicly?' you have to scroll through everything.

## Current state (verified 2026-04-07)
The **backend** at views.py:352-360 already supports a visibility filter:
\`\`\`python
visibility_filter = request.args.get('visibility')
if visibility_filter:
    query += ' AND visibility = ?'
    params.append(visibility_filter)
\`\`\`
So `https://listhub.globalbr.ai/dashboard?visibility=public` works *today* — but only if you know to type it.

The **dashboard.html template** does NOT expose this. No filter buttons. (Verified: `grep -n 'visibility_filter\|visibility=' templates/dashboard.html` returns nothing.)

## Proposed UI
On the dashboard, add a filter chip row above the item list:
\`[ All ] [ Public ] [ Unlisted ] [ Shared ] [ Private ]\`
- Active chip highlighted, others muted
- Clicks set the `?visibility=` query param and reload (or AJAX)
- Chip labels also show counts: \`Public (12)\`, \`Private (47)\`, etc.

## Why this is small
- Backend: done
- Template: ~30 lines of Jinja2 + a tiny CSS block
- Counts: one extra query

## Related
- code-6gy (P2) — visibility badges on items in lists/dashboards. The badges are the per-item indicator; this filter is the per-view aggregate. They go together.
- Could be bundled into the same PR as code-6gy.
close_reason: Dashboard visibility filter UI already existed, badges updated

title: Add recently edited lists section to ListHub dashboard
ListHub: Add llms.txt and agent discovery standards
description: Add llms.txt (https://llmstxt.org/) and other agent/LLM discovery standards to ListHub. This makes the API discoverable by AI agents without prior knowledge. Could also include: agents.json, .well-known/ai-plugin.json, OpenAPI spec, MCP server manifest. The goal is that any agent can discover ListHub's capabilities just by checking standard files at the root.

title: Import flowtools.directory into ListHub
description:
Import the flowtools.directory content into ListHub. Flowtools.directory is a curated directory of tools for thought / knowledge management / PKM tools.

Steps:
- Scrape or export the directory entries from flowtools.directory
- Import them as ListHub items with structured metadata (tool name, URL, description, category, tags)

This is linked to listhub-ogp (the publishable curated directory UI) — the flowtools import would serve as the first real content powering that directory view. Also linked to listhub-bd1 (view templates) for how the imported data gets displayed.

title: Activity pulse / recent updates view
description:
## Goal
Show a pulse/activity feed of recent updates across ListHub — what's been created, edited, who's active. Makes the site feel alive and helps users discover new content.

## Ideas to Explore

### Activity Feed (GitHub-style)
- Chronological feed of recent public activity: "alice created Best AI Tools", "bob updated Reading List 2026"
- Could live at /activity or /pulse as its own page
- Or embedded as a section on the homepage or people page

### Per-User Activity
- On each user's profile: show recent activity (created X, updated Y)
- Helps visitors understand what someone is actively working on
- Already have `last_active` from MAX(updated_at) — could show more granular timeline

### Pulse Dashboard
- GitHub Insights-style overview: how many lists created this week, active contributors, trending lists
- Could show graphs of activity over time
- Most edited lists (high revision counts = actively maintained)

### People Page Integration
- The people page now sorts by "Recently Active" — could show more context about *what* they were active on
- Mini activity snippet: "last updated: AI Tools list (2 hours ago)"

## Implementation Notes
- All data already exists (item.updated_at, item.created_at, item_version for edit history)
- No new tables needed — just queries and a new template
- Could start simple: a page that shows the last 50 public item creates/edits with timestamps and usernames
- item_version table has per-edit history if we want detailed "what changed" view

title: Agent-friendly search surface across all ListHub items
description:
## Question to think about first
**Does agent search even make sense as a dedicated feature, or should we just expose git-clone/list-items and let the agent walk the tree?**

This is the fundamental design question. There are two coherent models:

### Model A: Filesystem-first (the "agents are coding agents" view)
- Agents clone the repo (git clone over HTTPS with API key)
- They walk directories, grep markdown, read files directly
- No dedicated search endpoint needed — git + grep is the interface
- This matches how Claude Code works with any codebase
- Pros: simple, agent-friendly in a boring way, no new API surface
- Cons: requires clone (large repos slow), no relevance ranking, no cross-user search

### Model B: API-first (the "agents are apps" view)
- Agents call `GET /api/v1/search?q=...` and get structured, ranked results
- Server does FTS5 relevance ranking, visibility filtering, pagination
- No clone needed; works for agents that don't have filesystem access
- Pros: fast, remote-friendly, doesn't require git
- Cons: we own a search index that needs to stay correct; another endpoint to maintain

### Probably: both
- `GET /api/v1/search` already exists for Model B (keyword FTS5, owner-scoped)
- Add `scope=public|mine|all` parameter to expose cross-user search
- Add `GET /api/v1/search/semantic?q=...` later for embeddings-based retrieval
- Keep git-clone access as the "walk everything" backup
- Document both in llms.txt so agents know their options

## Concrete improvements to existing search

Current state: `GET /api/v1/search?q=X` returns items **owned by the authenticated user only**. For an agent to find public content across the whole site, it doesn't work.

Changes needed:
1. **Add `scope` parameter**: `mine` (default, current behavior), `public` (only public items, no auth required), `all` (authenticated user's items + all public)
2. **Include `owner_username` in results** so agents can construct URLs
3. **Include a `matched_snippet` field** with FTS5 highlight so the agent sees why something matched
4. **Pagination**: `page` and `per_page` query params (current returns up to 100)
5. **Filter by type/tag/visibility** combinators in one call
6. **Response shape stable and versioned**: document the schema in llms.txt

## Document agent search in llms.txt

The current llms.txt mentions `GET /api/v1/search?q=:query` but doesn't explain:
- That it's owner-scoped (big gotcha for agents looking for public content)
- How to clone the git repo as an alternative
- When to prefer which

Update llms.txt with a "How to find things" section that explicitly discusses both paths.

## Semantic search (future)

FTS5 keyword search is fine for developer-style agents that know the exact terms. For natural-language agents ("find me that note about Dr. Alderman and PRP injections"), embeddings-based semantic search is dramatically better.

Plan:
- Generate OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small` vectors for each item on write
- Store in a `item_embedding` table (SQLite blob, or an actual vector DB later)
- Add `GET /api/v1/search/semantic?q=...` that does cosine similarity
- Fall back to FTS5 if embeddings aren't available

Defer this to V2. V1 = fix the scope bug and document the existing endpoint properly.

## Related
- `listhub-95u` — Global search UI (uses this API underneath)
- `listhub-3wv` — Search-or-create (layered on top)
- Existing `GET /api/v1/search` in `api.py`
- `llms.txt` discovery doc (needs updating)

title: Move visibility/edit permissions controls to top of list page
description:
## Problem
Currently, visibility and edit permissions (private/public/public_edit/shared) are only accessible in the edit form, buried below the content editor. When viewing your own list, there's no quick way to toggle permissions without going into full edit mode.

## Design Goals
- As the list owner, I should see visibility/permissions controls at the top of the list view page
- One-click toggle between private, public (view only), and public (anyone can edit)
- Should feel lightweight — not a full settings panel, more like a quick dropdown or segmented control
- Non-owners should see a read-only badge showing current visibility (already partially there with badges)

## Possible Designs

### Option A: Inline dropdown in header
Next to the list title on /@username/slug, show a small dropdown:
`[🔒 Private ▾]` → click → `Private | Public | Public Edit | Shared (WIP)`
Saves immediately via API call, no page reload needed.

### Option B: Segmented control bar
A thin bar below the title with toggle buttons:
`🔒 Private | 🌐 Public | ✏️ Anyone Can Edit`
Active state highlighted. Click to switch.

### Option C: Settings gear icon
Small ⚙️ icon next to title that expands a mini-panel with:
- Visibility toggle
- Share settings
- Slug/URL display
- Edit button

## Scope
- Start with owner's view only (when viewing your own list at /@you/slug)
- Later: could extend to dashboard list view (quick toggle per list row)
- API already supports `PUT /api/v1/items/:id` with visibility field — just need the frontend

title: Import curated noos knowledge documents into ListHub
description:
## Goal

title: Git history viewer for user repos
description:
Add a way to see the git history of each user's items — what changed, when, diffs. Key question: how does it interplay with per-item visibility and `<!-- private -->` content blocks inside public items?

## Options considered

1. **Roll our own** (recommended). Render `git log` + diffs in the web UI using pygit2 or shelling out. Simplest, full control. Show on `/@user/item` page as a "History" tab, plus a repo-level history view at `/@user/history`.
2. **Use Forgejo/Gitea**: self-hosted GitHub clone. Heavyweight but gets PRs, issues, web editor for free. Overkill.
3. **Auto-push to GitHub**: mirror each user's bare repo to `listhub-USERNAME`. Free GitHub UI. But user repos contain private items — would need filter. Not worth the GitHub management overhead.

## Recommendation: roll our own, and ride on top of `listhub-xtc.2`

The public-mirror architecture in `listhub-xtc.2` **collapses the privacy problem for non-owner views**. Instead of trying to filter git log output live:

- **Non-owner view** → point the history UI at the `<username>.public.git` mirror. `git log`, diffs, and blame are all safe by construction because the public mirror already has: (a) private/shared items excluded entirely, (b) `<!-- private -->...<!-- /private -->` blocks stripped from included items before the commit was made.
- **Owner view** → point at the full `<username>.git` bare repo (auth required). Owner sees everything including their own private blocks.

This is **much simpler** than trying to filter history on the fly.

## Privacy interplay — the tricky cases

| Scenario | Behavior |
|---|---|
| Item currently public, was public yesterday, non-owner viewing | Shows git log from public mirror. Normal case. |
| Item currently private, non-owner viewing | Item isn't in the public mirror at all → 404. No history visible, no hint the item ever existed. |
| Item currently public but was private last week (became public) | Public mirror only has commits from *after* it became public. Earlier private history invisible to non-owners. |
| Item currently public but was public last week, then private, then public again | Public mirror has a gap (commits during the private period were skipped). Non-owner sees two "islands" of history with a gap. Owner sees the full continuous history. |
| Item currently public and has `<!-- private -->` blocks | Public mirror's diffs show the scrubbed version; owner's view shows the raw version. Diff between adjacent commits may look different to owner vs non-owner. |
| Item currently public, was edited to add a `<!-- private -->` block | Non-owner's diff shows "no change" for the private region (it was never there in the public mirror). Owner's diff shows the added block. |
| Item currently public, was edited to *remove* a `<!-- private -->` block | Non-owner's diff shows no change. Owner's diff shows the removed block. |
| Currently public folder, historical commits from when it was private (once listhub-74j ships) | Same principle: public mirror only has commits from when the folder was public. |

**Rule of thumb**: the non-owner's history view is "what the public mirror shows". It is consistent with the current public visibility state and never leaks anything that wasn't in the public mirror at commit time. The owner sees ground truth.

## Non-owner UI elements

- `/@user/history` — repo-level commit log (paginated). Author, timestamp, commit message, list of files touched.
- `/@user/<slug>/history` — item-level log. Only commits that touched this file.
- `/@user/<slug>/history/<sha>` — diff view for a single commit on this file.
- Blame view: `/@user/<slug>/blame` — line-by-line "last-modified" attribution.
- **Gaps are visible**: if the public mirror has a gap (item was private for a while), show a subtle "some history not shown (visibility changed)" marker. Don't hide the gap; surface it honestly.

## Owner UI elements

Everything above, but against the full bare repo. Plus:
- Toggle: "Show as public viewer sees it" — renders the owner's own history through the public mirror. Useful for previewing what external cloners see.
- Diff highlighting: lines inside `<!-- private -->` blocks are marked in the owner view so it's clear which parts won't appear in public history.

## Data model note

The strategy doc (`~/memory/projects/listhub-strategy-2026-04-08.md`) concluded `item_version` is dead weight (1.04 versions per item, git is already the history). **Drop `item_version` as part of this ticket** — the git history viewer should read directly from the bare repos, not from the SQLite table.

## Dependencies / interplay

- **Blocked by listhub-xtc.2** (public-only git mirror architecture) — without it, non-owner history views have to filter on the fly, which is much harder
- **Relates to listhub-74j** — folder visibility cascade affects what the public mirror contains
- **Supersedes the `item_version` table** — this ticket includes dropping it

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `/@user/<slug>/history` shows commit log for the item
- [ ] Non-owner only sees commits from the public mirror
- [ ] Owner sees commits from the full bare repo, with an optional "public view" toggle
- [ ] Diffs honor the mirror they're read from (no private-block leakage to non-owners)
- [ ] Gaps in public history (from visibility transitions) are marked, not hidden
- [ ] Private items 404 the history endpoint for non-owners
- [ ] Repo-level history page `/@user/history` works for both perspectives
- [ ] `item_version` table is dropped as part of this work
- [ ] Tested with a contrived item that transitioned private→public→private→public → verify the gap handling

## Related
- listhub-xtc.2 — public-only git mirror architecture (the blocker that makes this tractable)
- listhub-xtc — GitHub-like repo UX epic (parent)
- listhub-74j — folder-level visibility
- listhub-a3e — ZIP download (shares public mirror)
- listhub-ev1 — clone URL + download button
- `~/memory/projects/listhub-strategy-2026-04-08.md` — `item_version` is dead weight analysis

title: Auto-push user repos to GitHub + encrypted items architecture
description:
## Goal
Automatically mirror ListHub user repositories (especially the community directory) to GitHub so content is easily browsable, forkable, and discoverable via GitHub search/UI.

## Motivation
- GitHub provides free browsing UI, markdown rendering, search, and PR-based contribution
- Community directories become much more accessible when on GitHub
- Git history already exists in ListHub — just need to push it upstream
- Related to listhub-j47 (git browsing on ListHub itself) but complementary

## Implementation Options

### Option A: Auto-push public repos to GitHub
- On each commit to a ListHub repo, push to a corresponding GitHub repo
- Could use GitHub org per ListHub user, or a single org with user-prefixed repos
- Trigger: post-commit hook or periodic cron sync
- Trade-offs: simple, great discoverability, but all content must be truly public

### Option B: Encrypted items within public repos
- Push repos to GitHub but encrypt specific line items or files marked as private/unlisted
- Uses age/GPG encryption per-item, with keys managed by ListHub
- Browsing on GitHub shows cleartext for public items, ciphertext blobs for private ones
- Trade-offs: more complex key management, but enables single repo with public+private

### Option C: Full encryption-first architecture
- ALL repos go to GitHub (one per user)
- Public items cleartext, private items encrypted at rest
- ListHub holds decryption keys and handles access control
- Supports encrypted line items as a first-class feature in the data model
- Trade-offs: most flexible but requires robust key management, rotation, sharing

## Trade-off Analysis
| Concern | Option A | Option B | Option C |
|---------|----------|----------|----------|
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Discoverability | Excellent | Good | Good |
| Privacy | Public only | Mixed | Mixed |
| Key management | None | Per-item | Per-item + sharing |
| Git diff readability | Full | Partial | Partial |
| Contribution via PR | Easy | Awkward | Awkward |

## Recommendation
Start with Option A for community/public directories (quick win). Evaluate Option B/C for user repos with mixed visibility later. Encrypted items are valuable long-term but need careful design — item-level (content field) vs file-level (entire .md files).

## Open Questions
- Which GitHub org/account to push to?
- Opt-in vs default for public repos?
- For encrypted items: age vs GPG vs libsodium sealed boxes?
- How to handle PRs that touch encrypted items?

title: File bucket / upload architecture — decide where binary files live (Noos bucket vs local vs S3)
description:
## Goal
Decide where binary files (images, PDFs, audio, video) live for ListHub uploads. This is foundational for image support (listhub-c6x) and any future non-markdown content.

## Options

### Option A: Local disk under `LISTHUB_BLOB_ROOT`
- Pros: simple, no external dependency, fast, easy to back up with git or rsync
- Cons: doesnt scale to multiple app servers, prod disk needs to grow

### Option B: AWS S3 / Cloudflare R2
- Pros: scales, CDN-friendly, cheap at ListHub volumes
- Cons: one more service to manage, signed-URL complexity for private files, network hop latency

### Option C: Noos bucket
- Pros: reuses existing Noos infrastructure, aligns with the noos-as-knowledge-graph vision
- Cons: requires Noos to have file-storage primitives (does it today?), couples ListHub to Noos
- Question: is there an existing `noos` file bucket/blob API we can dogfood?

### Option D: IPFS / content-addressed distributed storage
- Pros: philosophically aligned with "Git-backed open knowledge" ethos, permanent
- Cons: operational complexity, retrieval latency, pinning cost

## Decision criteria
- How much is the user expected to upload in v1? (Probably MBs, not GBs.)
- Is ListHub expected to stay single-server for the foreseeable future?
- Do we want private files to require auth on every fetch?
- How important is dedup across users?

## Recommendation (to validate)
Start with **Option A (local disk)**, content-addressed by sha256. Migrate to R2/S3 when the disk fills up or when multi-server scaling becomes a need. Use signed URLs (HMAC with a secret) for private file access.

## Investigate first
- Does Noos already have a file bucket/blob API? If yes, prototype against it.
- What does Mew currently do for attachments?

## Related
- listhub-c6x — inline image support (blocked on this decision)
- listhub-4jm — multi-sheet CSV (related non-markdown content problem)

title: Inline permissions and quick actions on item view
description:
## Problem
When viewing a list/item (not in edit mode), there's no way to quickly change its visibility or edit permissions. You have to click Edit, change the dropdown, and save — even if you just want to toggle between public and public_edit.

More broadly, the item viewing experience lacks inline controls that an owner (or authorized user) should have without entering full edit mode.

## Proposed Features

### 1. Inline visibility/permission toggle on item view
- Owner sees a clickable visibility badge on the item page (e.g., clicking "public" shows dropdown: private, public, public_edit)
- One click to change — no need to enter edit mode
- Same pattern as GitHub's repo visibility toggle

### 2. Quick actions on item cards (profile/dashboard/explore)
- Hover reveals action buttons: Edit, Share, Change Visibility
- Or: right-click context menu
- Or: "..." overflow menu on each card

### 3. Public edit items should show edit permission clearly
- When viewing a public_edit item as a non-owner, the "Edit" button should be prominent
- Show who last edited and how many contributors
- Consider: "N people have edited this" counter

### 4. Comprehensive UX audit for inline controls

**Owner viewing their own item should be able to:**
- [x] See current visibility (badge exists)
- [ ] Change visibility without entering edit mode
- [ ] Share with specific users without entering edit mode  
- [ ] See edit history / version count
- [ ] Quick-delete (with confirmation)
- [ ] Duplicate/fork item

**Non-owner viewing a public_edit item should see:**
- [x] Edit button (exists in item.html)
- [ ] Who last edited
- [ ] Number of contributors/edits
- [ ] "Fork to my account" if they want their own copy instead of editing in-place

**Non-owner viewing a public (view-only) item should see:**
- [ ] "Save/bookmark" button (covered by listhub-sxu)
- [ ] "Fork to my account" to create their own copy
- [ ] "Suggest edit" (like GitHub's fork-and-PR model — future)

### 5. Settings/permissions panel
- Could be a slide-out panel or modal instead of a full page reload
- Shows: visibility, sharing, edit history, API access info
- Accessible via gear icon on the item page

## Related Tickets
- listhub-60j: Consolidate edit experiences (shared infrastructure)
- listhub-sxu: Save/favorite lists (bookmark button)
- listhub-8wi: Per-user sharing with write permission
- listhub-z0t: Make lists publicly editable (public_edit visibility)
- listhub-2lg: Git history viewer (edit history)

## Priority
Start with inline visibility toggle for owners (highest impact, simplest change). Then add the fork/duplicate action. The full permissions panel is a bigger effort.

title: Folder → Wiki promotion: upgrade path from item folder to first-class wiki with own git repo
description:
## Goal
Let a user or admin 'promote' an existing folder of items into a first-class wiki with its own bare git repo, edit policy, and URL.

## Why
listhub-a2o defines wikis as first-class objects (own git repo, edit policy, slug). listhub-wn4 defines folder views. This ticket bridges them: a folder that gets popular enough earns wiki status without the user having to recreate everything from scratch.

## Promotion flow
1. User has a folder like /@jacobreal/chronicpain/ with 20 items
2. User (or admin) clicks 'Promote to wiki'
3. System creates a new bare git repo, exports all items as markdown+frontmatter, sets up the wiki table row
4. Old folder items get a redirect or are marked as 'migrated to wiki'
5. The wiki now has its own edit policy, sidebar tree, clone URL

## Edge cases
- Items in the folder with mixed visibility → wiki ACL inherits per-item visibility
- Sub-folders → become paths in the wiki repo
- Items that are cross-referenced from outside the folder → redirects

## Related
- listhub-a2o — wikis as first-class objects (the destination)
- listhub-wn4 — folder view (the source)
- listhub-bqe — homepage curation (promoted wikis are prime candidates for featuring)

title: Search-or-create: fallback to new article when no results
description:
## Goal
When the user searches for something that doesn't exist yet, offer to create a new item with that title inline — no need to switch to "New item" flow.

This is the "search-or-create" pattern (Notion, Roam, Obsidian all do variants). It turns the search bar into a universal entry point: search to find, or search to create.

## Behavior

1. User types "research on karpathy" in the global search bar (Cmd+K)
2. Search returns 0 matches (or matches that aren't what the user wanted)
3. At the bottom of the results, a suggestion: **"Create new item: 'research on karpathy'"**
4. Click → create a new private item with that title (slug auto-generated) and open it in the editor
5. No intermediate forms, no confirmation dialogs, no folder pickers (put it at the root; user can move it later)

## Why this matters

- Lowest-friction "I had an idea, capture it now" path
- Works naturally with voice dictation / quick thought flows
- Parallels the agent-first pattern (just state intent, agent creates)
- Particularly useful for LLM wikis where items are cheap and numerous

## Open questions

**Should the created item default to private?** Yes — inherit the user's default visibility (usually private). User can change after.

**What folder does it go in?** Root by default. If the user was on a folder page when they searched, maybe offer "in {current folder}" as a second option.

**Should it be a separate button or just the last row in the results list?** Last row in results is lower-friction. Always visible, Enter to trigger.

**Agent integration**: should the API offer a search-or-create endpoint too? `POST /api/v1/search-or-create {q: "..."}` returns either the best match or a newly created item. Could be useful for agents but arguably just two calls (search + new) is fine.

## Related
- `listhub-95u` — Global search (this builds on that)
- `listhub-16c` — Agent search (agent analog)

## Sequencing
This is intentionally tagged P3 because it depends on the global search (listhub-95u) landing first. After that this is a small additive change: one extra row in the results list + one handler.

title: Autocomplete search of existing lists when creating new list
description:
## Goal
When creating a new list, provide autocomplete/semantic search of all existing list titles (both your own and public lists). Helps avoid duplicates and discover related content.

## UX
- Text input field at the top of the "New List" form (or integrated into the title field)
- As user types, show a dropdown of matching existing lists (fuzzy/FTS search)
- Results include: title, owner (@username), visibility badge
- Clicking a result opens it in a sidebar panel (split view) so you can reference it while creating
- Should search both your own lists AND all public lists
- Debounced search (200-300ms) to avoid hammering the API

## Implementation Options
1. **Client-side**: Fetch all public list titles on page load, filter in JS (fast but doesn't scale)
2. **API endpoint**: New `GET /api/v1/search/titles?q=...` that searches just titles (lightweight)
3. **Reuse existing search**: Use `GET /api/v1/search?q=...` but add a `titles_only` param

## Sidebar
- When you click a search result, open it in a right sidebar (similar to GitHub's file preview)
- Sidebar shows rendered content of the selected list
- Can close sidebar to go back to full-width editor
- Sidebar should be resizable or at least a reasonable split (e.g., 60/40)

## Related
- Could eventually become a general "reference panel" feature for any editing context

title: Agent onboarding skill / much clearer llms.txt instructions for self-signup
description:
## Problem
2026-04-07 onboarding test: a coding agent took multiple steps and several false starts to figure out how to sign up for ListHub programmatically. The /llms.txt has the instructions but they are not concise enough or actionable enough for a fresh agent.

## Goal
Make agent onboarding take ~3 lines of code and never require the agent to web-log-in.

## Two parallel approaches

### Approach A: Improve /llms.txt to be agent-actionable
Restructure llms.txt so the FIRST section after the intro is a "Sign up in 30 seconds" recipe that any agent can paste into curl/python/whatever:

```
## Sign up as a new account (one call)
curl -X POST https://listhub.globalbr.ai/api/v1/auth/register \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"username": "your-username", "password": "yourpass1234"}'

# Save the returned `key` field. Use it as Bearer token for everything else.
# If you lose the key:
curl -X POST https://listhub.globalbr.ai/api/v1/auth/token \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"username": "your-username", "password": "yourpass1234"}'
```

### Approach B: Build a Claude Code skill for ListHub onboarding
A user-installable skill that triggers when the user says "set up ListHub" or "sign me up for ListHub". The skill:
1. Asks for a desired username (defaults to the human user's name)
2. Generates a password
3. POSTs to /auth/register
4. Saves the returned key to ~/.listhub/credentials or similar
5. Reports success with a link to the new profile

This is the ideal experience but requires building + distributing a skill.

## Recommendation
Ship A first (just edit llms.txt). Then build B as a follow-up.

## Specific llms.txt improvements needed
- Move the "register" example to the very top of the agent section
- Add the recovery flow inline so agents see it before they need it
- Add a "common mistakes" section: do not try to web-log-in, the API is the only path
- Add a working python snippet (not just curl)
- Add a "tool definition" snippet for OpenAI/Anthropic tool-calling format

## Related
- 2026-04-07 onboarding test
- listhub-r62 (agent-native Drive replacement) — needs smooth onboarding
- listhub-7aw (agent-first web epic)
- listhub-bwl (API key recovery)
- listhub-r7u (API key web login)

title: Multi-sheet CSV/spreadsheet support for list items
description:
## Context
Body Masters was imported from a Google Sheets export as a single markdown table (70 rows × 7 columns). The original sheet has **additional sheets/tabs** (referenced in the export filename: "Main list.csv" implies others exist). Only the Main list was exported.

## Problem
ListHub items are single markdown documents. There's no native concept of "a list with multiple sheets/tabs." For spreadsheets with related-but-distinct tables (Main list, Archive, Notes, Metadata, etc.), we force users to either:
1. Merge everything into one big doc (loses structure)
2. Create multiple separate items (loses the "this is one conceptual thing" relationship)

## Proposed solutions (pick one or combine)

### Option A: Folder-as-spreadsheet convention
A spreadsheet = a folder. Each sheet = an `index.md` section or a file within that folder. The folder itself is an item with `item_type: "spreadsheet"` and children are auto-rendered as tabs in the UI.

### Option B: Multi-section markdown
Single markdown doc with `## Sheet Name` headings. Each H2 section is treated as a sheet when importing/exporting to CSV. Cleaner file model but loses per-sheet metadata.

### Option C: First-class sheets in the data model
Add a `sheet` table joined to `item`. Each sheet has its own rows/columns stored as JSON or as a child markdown table. Most flexible, most work.

### Option D: Import tooling only
Don't add any new data model. Just ship a CLI that imports a multi-sheet CSV/XLSX and creates N linked items with cross-references in the frontmatter.

## Recommendation
Start with **Option D** (tooling only). Ship `listhub-import-sheets` that:
1. Reads a multi-sheet CSV/XLSX export
2. Creates one item per sheet with `file_path = spreadsheet-name/sheet-name.md`
3. Links them via a top-level `spreadsheet-name/index.md` that lists all sheets
4. Preserves original sheet order

This gives us multi-sheet support without schema changes. Upgrade to Option A or C later if usage justifies it.

## Related
- Body Masters import (current Main list only): `~/code/DataExports/body-masters.md`
- Google Sheets → CSV multi-sheet export: Takeout splits by tab automatically
- Obsidian's approach: each sheet is just a separate file, no special UI
- Notion's approach: database with views, first-class data model

## Open questions
- Should the markdown table be editable in-browser with a spreadsheet-ish UI (Handsontable, Luckysheet)? Big lift, but makes the "Google Sheets replacement" story real.
- XLSX support, or CSV only? CSV is trivial, XLSX needs a library.
- Formulas? (Probably no, ever. Trap.)

title: Create Python/JS client library for assistant integration
Keep API docs and GUI in sync for sharing/visibility

title: Consolidate edit experiences: directory, normal, and public edit
description:
## Problem
The directory edit experience and the normal item edit experience are completely different implementations with different UIs, creating inconsistency and maintenance burden.

### Current state — three separate edit experiences:

**1. Normal item edit (edit.html)**
- Milkdown rich-text WYSIWYG editor with raw toggle
- Title, slug, type, visibility, tags fields
- Markdown preview
- Full metadata controls

**2. Directory edit (directory_edit.html)**
- Plain textarea only (no rich editor)
- No title/metadata fields (just raw markdown)
- Commit message field (git-aware)
- Completely separate template and route

**3. Public edit (edit.html in public_edit_mode)**
- Reuses edit.html with some fields hidden (slug, type, visibility)
- Shows "you are editing as @user" banner
- Almost the same as normal edit but with restrictions

### What's inconsistent:
- Directory edit has NO rich editor (Milkdown) — just a plain textarea
- Directory edit has NO preview capability
- Directory edit doesn't show the file path in a useful way
- Normal edit doesn't have a commit message field (useful for version history)
- Three different code paths for what is conceptually "editing markdown content"

## Proposed Fix
Consolidate to a single edit experience that adapts to context:

1. **Use edit.html (with Milkdown) for ALL editing** — including directory pages
2. **Context-aware fields:**
   - Normal items: show title, slug, type, visibility, tags
   - Directory pages: show file path, commit message. Hide slug/type/visibility
   - Public edit: show the "editing as" banner. Hide slug/type/visibility
3. **Delete directory_edit.html** after migration
4. **Single route handler** that delegates to the right backend (DB vs git repo)

## Other consolidation items found:

### Visibility handling
- `public_edit` visibility is referenced inconsistently — some queries check `IN ('public', 'public_edit')` while others only check `'public'`
- landing.html only shows `visibility = 'public'` items (line 96 of views.py) — should also include `public_edit`
- The explore page (line 151) does include both

### Edit permission checks
- item.html checks `can_edit` variable, but profile.html checks `is_owner or visibility == 'public_edit'` separately
- Should be a single `can_edit` helper used everywhere

### Template duplication
- profile.html and dash.html both implement folder tree views with slightly different logic
- Tree rendering should be a shared partial/include

## Priority
Start with the directory edit → Milkdown migration (biggest UX gap), then consolidate the smaller items.

title: Bulk folder/vault import UI — drag a folder into the browser
description:
## Goal
A drag-drop folder upload UI that walks a local folder client-side and uploads each file to ListHub, preserving structure.

## Flow
1. User drags a folder (e.g. their Obsidian vault, a Drive folder export, a downloaded zip extraction) into a drop zone on /dash/upload
2. Client-side JS walks the folder via the Filesystem Access API or drag-drop file tree
3. For each file:
   - .md → use upload endpoint (listhub-ma7)
   - .docx/.doc/.rtf → convert via pandoc (listhub-dwa)
   - Image → upload as attachment (listhub-c6x)
   - Other → upload as binary attachment
4. Preserve folder hierarchy via file_path on each created item
5. Show a progress UI (X of Y files)
6. Optionally: bulk visibility setting (set all imported items to private/public/...)

## Why
This is the counterpart to the already-shipped server-side import script (`scripts/import_markdown.py`). That script is for power users with terminal access. This is for everyone else.

## Tech notes
- Browser Filesystem Access API is Chromium-only (no Safari yet) — fallback to drag-drop
- Need to handle large folders (100+ files) without locking up the browser — queue with throttled concurrency (5-10 at a time)
- Consider client-side ZIP import: drag a .zip, extract in browser via JSZip, process each file

## Related
- listhub-r62 — epic
- listhub-ma7, listhub-dwa, listhub-c6x — per-file-type upload
- listhub-74j — folder visibility (set for imported folders)
- `scripts/import_markdown.py` — existing server-side equivalent

title: Folder-level visibility with cascade (Drive-style folder permissions)
description:
## Vision
Folders in ListHub should be first-class entities with their own visibility, and items inside should inherit that visibility by default. Same model as Google Drive folder permissions.

## Current state
Folders are implicit — derived from item file_path at query time. No `folder` table, no per-folder metadata. Visibility is per-item only.

## Recommended approach: Option B (metadata files, git-native)
Rather than adding a `folder` table, store folder metadata in a `.folder.yaml` file at each folder level in the user git repo:

```yaml
# chronicpain/.folder.yaml
visibility: public
title: Chronic Pain Resources
description: Bay Area practitioners I have worked with
```

The tree builder reads this overlay on top of the existing file_path derivation. Items with no explicit visibility inherit from the nearest ancestor folder. Items with explicit visibility override.

### Why Option B
- **Zero schema migration** — existing items keep working
- **Git-native** — permissions survive clone/push/pull automatically
- **Agent-friendly** — agents can write `.folder.yaml` like any other markdown file
- **Upgrade-compatible** — if we ever want query caching, add a folder table that indexes these files

### Alternative options rejected
- **Option A: first-class folder table** — cleanest data model but biggest upfront cost; backfill from existing file_paths; needs git sync adapter for `.folder.yaml` anyway
- **Option C: extend item_tag with folder properties** — hacky, no proper inheritance

## Design decisions to make
- [ ] **Cascade mode**: soft inherit (item explicit visibility wins) vs hard cascade (folder overwrites items on change). I recommend SOFT.
- [ ] **Bulk change action**: a separate "apply to all items in folder" action that DOES hard-cascade on user request
- [ ] **Default for new items** in a folder: inherit from folder (so new items in a public folder default to public)
- [ ] **Conflict resolution**: explicit item visibility always wins over folder default
- [ ] **Empty folders**: can still have a visibility to serve as default for future children
- [ ] **Metadata files themselves**: treat `.folder.yaml` like `.gitignore` — not listed, not rendered, always loaded server-side
- [ ] **Migration path**: for existing folders that have implicit visibility aggregated from children, optionally auto-create `.folder.yaml` with the inferred visibility on first edit

## Implementation sketch

### 1. Tree builder overlay (30 lines)
```python
def load_folder_metadata(owner_username, folder_path):
    # Read /git/<user>.git/<folder_path>/.folder.yaml via _git_read
    raw = _git_read(user_repo, "show", f"HEAD:{folder_path}/.folder.yaml")
    return yaml.safe_load(raw) if raw else None
```

### 2. API endpoints
- `GET /api/v1/folders/:path` → return metadata
- `PUT /api/v1/folders/:path` → set metadata (creates the `.folder.yaml` file via a commit on the users bare repo)
- `PUT /api/v1/folders/:path/visibility` → shortcut for the common case
- `POST /api/v1/folders/:path/apply-visibility` → bulk cascade to all items in the folder

### 3. Effective visibility resolver
When rendering an item or deciding access, resolve its effective visibility by walking up ancestors until one has an explicit setting. Cache per-request.

### 4. UI
- Right-click / hover action on folder rows: "Set folder visibility"
- Folder header on `/@user/path/` zoomed folder page: visibility badge + quick toggle
- Sidebar folder rows: visibility icon already renders via the aggregation logic from earlier, but now reads from metadata instead of aggregating

## Vision alignment
This is the key unlock for "agent-native Google Drive replacement." Once folders have permissions, ListHub matches Drive on the most important permission primitive, while keeping the markdown-first advantage.

## Related
- listhub-r62 — Agent-native Google Drive replacement (epic this supports)
- listhub-ftr — Central home doc (home item lives at root, this pattern lets the root be "private folder with one public home item")
- listhub-f18 — Public/private home page alternatives (the <!-- private --> block is the line-level complement)
- listhub-2zv — File bucket architecture
- listhub-c6x — Inline image support

## No real blockers
This is purely design work plus a few hundred lines of code. The hardest part is agreeing on cascade semantics, not the implementation.

title: Rethink lists vs categories/folders naming and UI
description:
## Problem
The homepage says "Featured Lists" but those entries are really folders/categories from the community directory (tech, cooking, reading, etc.), not individual lists. This is confusing — a "list" implies a flat collection of items, while these are hierarchical categories containing multiple lists.

## Areas to Address

### 1. Homepage "Featured Lists" section
- These are community directory categories, not lists
- Consider renaming to "Featured Topics", "Categories", "Collections", or "Directories"
- Or rethink what goes there — maybe show actual popular lists alongside directory categories

### 2. Community directory folder icons
- Directory tree currently has plain text with triangle toggles
- Add folder icons (📁/📂) to make the hierarchy more visually obvious
- File icons (📄) for individual .md entries
- This helps distinguish folders from leaf items at a glance

### 3. Broader naming taxonomy
- "List" = a single user-created document (note, list, document)
- "Category" / "Folder" / "Topic" = a directory node in the community tree
- "Collection" = could mean a curated set of lists from multiple users
- Need consistent terminology across the site, API docs, and UI

### 4. Consider whether lists and categories are the same thing
- Right now they're separate systems: lists are DB items, categories are git folders
- Should a user be able to create a "category" that contains other people's lists?
- This touches on the broader question of whether ListHub is a wiki, a directory, or both

## Quick wins for tomorrow
- Rename "Featured Lists" on homepage
- Add folder/file icons to community directory tree
- Review all user-facing copy for list vs category confusion

title: Agent onboarding UX: one-click personal agent flow + standards research
description:
## Question
How do we make the "fully agent-friendly app" workflow even more obvious and frictionless? Is there a standard or emerging convention for this?

## Vision
A user visiting ListHub should see something like an "Open in your agent" button — they click it, and their personal AI agent (Claude, ChatGPT, Codex, etc.) is launched with the right context to register, get an API key, and start working with ListHub immediately.

## Brainstorm Areas

### 1. "Open in Agent" UX pattern
- Button on landing page / item pages: "Open in Claude" / "Open in ChatGPT" / "Open in your agent"
- What does this actually do? Options:
  - Copy a prompt to clipboard that the user pastes into their agent
  - Deep link into a specific agent (claude:// protocol? ChatGPT custom GPT link?)
  - Generate a pre-filled prompt URL (like ChatGPT share links)
  - Show a modal with "give these instructions to your agent" + copyable text
- The prompt would include: base URL, registration curl, what the agent can do

### 2. In-App Agent Sidebar
- ListHub itself could have an embedded "Your Agent" sidebar panel
- User connects their Claude/OpenAI API key in settings
- The sidebar IS their personal agent, with ListHub tools pre-loaded
- Think: Notion AI sidebar, but powered by the user's own agent
- Could start simple: text input → sends to Claude API with ListHub MCP tools
- Items on the current page become context for the agent automatically

### 3. Cortex as the Personal Agent Layer
- **Cortex** (github.com/IdeaFlowCo/cortex) is already a cloud-based Claude CLI platform
- Each user gets an isolated Linux workspace with Claude CLI pre-installed
- Has: real-time chat, file browser, web terminal, session continuity
- **The connection**: ListHub items could be the user's knowledge base inside Cortex
  - Cortex workspace auto-clones user's ListHub repo
  - Claude in Cortex gets ListHub MCP tools pre-configured
  - User's lists/notes/docs are always available as context
- **"Open in Cortex"** button on ListHub: launches your personal Claude agent with this list/item as context
- Cortex already has auth, provisioning, WebSocket chat — just needs ListHub integration
- CloudCLI (cortex/cloudcli) is a local-first variant that could also integrate

### 4. Existing standards to research
- **llms.txt** (already implemented) — machine-readable site description
- **agents.json** / **.well-known/ai-plugin.json** — OpenAI's ChatGPT plugin spec (deprecated but pattern exists)
- **MCP (Model Context Protocol)** — Anthropic's standard for tool servers. ListHub as MCP server = any Claude session gets native access
- **OpenAPI spec** — standard API description, agents can auto-generate tools from it
- **Tool Use schemas** — include recommended tool definitions in llms.txt or a dedicated endpoint
- **A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol** — Google's emerging standard
- **Agent Cards** — part of A2A, machine-readable agent capability descriptions

### 5. "Fully agent-friendly app" checklist
What makes an app truly agent-friendly? Draft a checklist:
- [x] Programmatic registration (no browser needed)
- [x] API key in one call
- [x] llms.txt
- [ ] OpenAPI spec (machine-parseable endpoint definitions)
- [ ] MCP server (agents can use ListHub as a tool natively)
- [ ] "Open in agent" button on the site
- [ ] In-app agent sidebar
- [ ] Agent-optimized error messages (structured JSON, actionable)
- [ ] Rate limit headers (so agents can self-throttle)
- [ ] Webhook/event support (so agents can react to changes)
- [ ] Cortex integration (personal agent with ListHub pre-loaded)

### 6. Could this become a standard itself?
ListHub could define and promote an "Agent-Friendly App" badge/standard:
- A spec that says "if your app has these N things, it's agent-friendly"
- A verification endpoint (like robots.txt but for agents)
- Could list agent-friendly apps in the ListHub directory itself
- The agent-friendly-apps list at /@jacobreal/agent-friendly-apps becomes the registry

## Open Questions
- Which agent platforms support deep linking / launch URLs?
- Is there a de facto "open in agent" protocol emerging?
- Should we build an MCP server for ListHub? (connects to listhub-3q6)
- What does the "Open in your agent" button actually look like in the UI?
- How deep should the Cortex ↔ ListHub integration go? Shared auth? Shared data layer?
- Should the in-app sidebar be its own feature or just link out to Cortex?

title: Pioneer the agent-first web: persistent agent bar, Chrome extension, standard
description:
## Vision
Pioneer the "agent-first web" — a new design paradigm where websites are built from the ground up for AI agents to use, with the human UI as a complementary layer. ListHub can be the first site to fully embody this.

## Core Concepts

### 1. Persistent Agent Bar
- A text input bar at the top of every page (like a search bar, but for agent commands)
- Connected to your personal Claude/Cortex agent via API
- Context-aware: knows what page you're on and suggests relevant actions
- "Add this to my list" / "Save this for later" / "Share with Alice"
- Replaces traditional UI flows with natural language

### 2. Agent as Browser Extension
- Chrome extension that provides a personal agent sidebar on ANY website
- Not per-app AI — YOUR agent that follows you across the web
- On agent-friendly sites (like ListHub), it auto-discovers APIs via llms.txt
- On regular sites, it can read the page content and take notes
- Connects to Cortex for persistent memory and session continuity

### 3. Context-Aware Actions
- Agent sees what page you're viewing and suggests relevant actions
- Viewing a list? "Would you like to fork this to your account?"
- Viewing someone's profile? "Save their best lists to your collection?"
- The page provides structured metadata that the agent can parse

### 4. Agent-First Design Principles (Manifesto)
- Every page is an API: if a human can do it, an agent can do it
- Self-service onboarding: no OAuth flows, no approval queues, one POST
- Your agent, everywhere: not per-app AI, your personal agent
- Context-aware: the agent knows where you are and what's relevant

### 5. Standard for Agent-Friendly Websites
- Could ListHub define a standard other sites adopt?
- llms.txt (discovery) + registration API + structured actions
- "Agent-Friendly" badge/certification
- Chrome extension could work with any site that follows the standard

## Implementation Phases

**Phase 1: Agent Bar on ListHub**
- Add the persistent input bar to the ListHub layout
- Wire it to the ListHub API (register, create, append, search)
- Start with client-side Claude API call (user provides their own key)

**Phase 2: Cortex Integration**
- Connect the agent bar to Cortex backend
- Shared session continuity — agent remembers previous conversations
- No API key needed — Cortex handles auth

**Phase 3: Chrome Extension**
- Build extension that adds the agent sidebar to any website
- Auto-detect agent-friendly sites via llms.txt
- Context extraction from page content
- Cortex as the backend for all agent actions

**Phase 4: Standard & Ecosystem**
- Document the agent-first web standard
- Build a registry of agent-friendly sites (on ListHub itself!)
- Provide badges/verification
- SDKs for other sites to become agent-friendly

## Related Work
- Mockup: /mockups/homepage-agentfirst.html
- listhub-78c: Agent onboarding UX brainstorm (related but narrower scope)
- listhub-3q6: llms.txt and agent discovery standards
- Cortex project: github.com/IdeaFlowCo/cortex

## Open Questions
- Build on top of MCP? Or independent protocol?
- Chrome extension vs native app vs both?
- Revenue model: free standard, paid Cortex hosting?
- How to handle sites that don't have llms.txt yet? (scraping? prompting?)

title: Standalone agent-first web standard site
description:
## Vision
A standalone, shareable website that defines and promotes the "Agent-First Web" standard. A place Jacob can send to founders, designers, and developers to show them what agent-first web design looks like and how to adopt it.

## What the site would include

### 1. The Standard
- Definition: what makes a website "agent-first"
- Checklist of required/recommended features
- Levels: Bronze (llms.txt + API), Silver (+ registration + MCP), Gold (+ agent bar + context-aware)

### 2. Live Examples
- Interactive mockups of agent-first sites (ListHub, ShelfLife/CookHub concepts)
- Side-by-side: traditional web vs agent-first web
- Screenshots/embeds showing the agent bar, sidebar, contextual actions

### 3. Install Your Agent
- Encouragement to install a desktop agent (Claude Code, Cortex)
- Chrome extension concept (agent sidebar on any site)
- "Try it now" — paste a URL and see how agent-friendly it is

### 4. For Developers
- How to make your site agent-first
- Add llms.txt (template generator)
- Add registration API (code examples)
- Add MCP server (tutorial)
- Badge/shield for README: "Agent-First Certified"

### 5. Registry
- Directory of agent-first sites (could pull from ListHub's agent-friendly-apps list)
- Submit your site for verification
- Leaderboard by agent-friendliness score

## Domain Ideas
- agentfirst.web (if available)
- agentfirstweb.com
- Could live at agentfirst.globalbr.ai initially
- Or as a section on renaissance.engineer

## Tech
- Static site (HTML/CSS/JS) — no backend needed initially
- Could deploy to GitHub Pages or Cloudflare Pages
- Pull examples from ListHub mockups

## Related
- listhub-7aw: Pioneer the agent-first web (epic)

title: Bi-directional link with llms-txt.io directory (788+ sites)
description:
## Goal
Bi-directional integration between ListHub's agent-friendly apps list and the llms-txt.io directory (788+ verified sites with llms.txt).

## Two Directions

### 1. Import: Pull llms-txt.io directory into ListHub
- Scrape/download the llms-txt.io/directory listing (788+ sites)
- Create a ListHub list or community directory section with these sites
- For each site: name, URL, llms.txt URL, category
- Periodically sync to pick up new additions
- Cross-reference with our /@jacobreal/agent-friendly-apps list
- Could auto-grade sites against our Bronze/Silver/Gold tiers by checking for: llms.txt (Bronze), registration API (Silver), agent bar (Gold)

### 2. Export: Get ListHub listed on llms-txt.io
- Submit ListHub to llms-txt.io/directory (we already have /llms.txt)
- Also submit to: llmstxt.site, directory.llmstxt.cloud
- Link from our agent-first standard page to these directories
- Get the "verified" badge from llms-txt.io

### 3. Cross-link the standards
- Our agent-first standard page should reference llms.txt as the Bronze tier foundation
- Link to llms-txt.io as the canonical directory for the content layer
- Our standard adds the Silver (self-service API) and Gold (agent-native UI) tiers on top
- Position our standard as complementary, not competing

## Implementation Ideas
- Scraper script that pulls llms-txt.io/directory and creates ListHub items
- A new community directory section: /directory/agent-friendly-sites/
- Auto-checker: given a URL, test for llms.txt, registration endpoint, MCP server
- Badge: sites in our directory get a tier rating (Bronze/Silver/Gold)

## Related

- listhub-3q6: llms.txt and agent discovery standards
