title: Google Drive connector: one-click import of a Drive folder into ListHub
description:
## Goal
One-click import of a Google Drive folder into ListHub, converting each Google Doc to markdown.

## Flow
1. User connects their Google account via OAuth (scope: drive.readonly + docs.readonly)
2. User picks a Drive folder from a picker UI
3. ListHub walks the folder recursively
4. For each Google Doc: export as .docx, convert to markdown via pandoc (listhub-dwa)
5. For each .md / .txt file: upload as-is
6. For each image: upload as attachment (listhub-c6x)
7. For each Sheet: export as CSV, create a markdown table item (listhub-4jm — multi-sheet CSV support)
8. Preserve folder hierarchy as ListHub folders (listhub-74j — folder visibility)
9. Default visibility: private

## Why this matters
This is THE differentiator for the "agent-native Drive replacement" pitch. Users can come from Drive, one-click their existing knowledge base over, and immediately get:
- Public URLs for anything they want to share
- Git access to everything
- Agent-editable markdown with YAML frontmatter
- Per-item and per-folder visibility the agent can set at write time

## Tech notes
- Google Drive API v3 + Google Docs API
- Need to handle: permissions (folders OAuth cant see), large files, rate limits, .gdoc shortcuts
- Conversion: Google Doc → .docx → markdown (via pandoc). Alternative: Google Doc → HTML → markdown via html2text or similar
- Incremental import: track drive_file_id on each imported item so future syncs only touch changed files

## Scope for v1
- One-time import (not continuous sync)
- Docs + markdown/text files only
- No sheets, no images (add in future passes)

## Related
- listhub-r62 — epic
- listhub-dwa — docx converter (dependency)
- listhub-ma7 — markdown file upload (dependency)
- listhub-c6x — image support (for future doc-with-images)
- listhub-4jm — multi-sheet CSV (for future Sheets import)
- listhub-74j — folder visibility (to preserve Drive folder permissions)

## Prior art
- Notion has a Google Drive importer (not great, lossy)
- Obsidian has community plugins for Drive sync (niche, unmaintained)
- Nobody has built an AGENT-NATIVE Drive importer that preserves the per-item visibility model ListHub has. This is a green field.

title: Per-user sharing with write permission
description: Share table already supports per-user sharing but permission is always read. Implement write permission.

title: Cmd+K quick switcher — Obsidian-style global/local search with filename or content mode
description:
## Goal
Add an Obsidian-style quick switcher / command palette triggered by Cmd+K (Ctrl+K on Linux/Windows) that opens a search overlay for instant navigation across items.

## Obsidian reference
In Obsidian, pressing Cmd+O (or Cmd+P for commands) opens a modal:
- Type to fuzzy-match across all note titles
- Arrow keys to navigate results
- Enter to open the selected note
- Escape to dismiss
- Optional: shift-enter for opening in new pane

ListHub should have an equivalent UX bound to Cmd+K (more standard across web apps — Linear, Slack, Notion, Raycast all use Cmd+K).

## Feature scope

### Trigger
- **Cmd+K** (Mac) / **Ctrl+K** (Win/Linux) from anywhere on the site → opens a search overlay
- Escape dismisses, arrow keys navigate, Enter opens

### Search UI
- Modal overlay centered on the viewport with:
  - Input box at the top (autofocused)
  - Results list below (max ~10 visible, scrollable if more)
  - Footer with hints: "↵ to open · ⌘↵ to open in new tab · esc to close"

### Search scope toggles (tabs or buttons)
1. **Local** — my items only (private + shared + public + public_edit I own)
2. **Global** — all public items across ListHub (public + public_edit + unlisted)
3. **Focused** — the currently focused user's items (if viewing /@user)
4. Remember last-used scope in localStorage

### Search mode toggles
1. **Titles / filenames only** — fast, matches slug + title (default)
2. **Full content** — slower, matches body text via FTS5

Toggle via a mode button or `/title` vs `/content` prefix in the input.

### Results
Each result row shows:
- Icon (folder/file, visibility badge)
- Title (matched characters highlighted)
- Path breadcrumb (@user/folder/path)
- Short content excerpt if content-mode matched
- Tags as chips

### Keyboard shortcuts
- `Cmd+K` — open
- `Esc` — close
- `↑` / `↓` — navigate
- `Enter` — open in current tab
- `Cmd+Enter` — open in new tab
- `Tab` — switch scope (Local → Global → Focused → Local)
- `/` — prefix to change mode (`/title query`, `/content query`, `/tag query`)

## Implementation sketch

### Frontend
- Overlay component: pure HTML/CSS + small JS (no framework needed)
- Listen for `keydown` globally, intercept Cmd+K
- Debounced fetch to `/api/v1/search/quick` on input change (150ms debounce)
- Keyboard nav: maintain a selectedIndex, scroll into view on arrow
- Result clicks + enters navigate via `window.location.href`

### Backend endpoint: `/api/v1/search/quick`
Query params:
- `q` — search string
- `scope` — `local` | `global` | `user:USERNAME`
- `mode` — `title` | `content`
- `limit` — default 10

Returns JSON:
```
[
  {
    "id": "...",
    "slug": "...",
    "title": "...",
    "url": "/@user/slug",
    "username": "...",
    "item_type": "document",
    "visibility": "public",
    "tags": [...],
    "excerpt": "matched content snippet if content mode"
  },
  ...
]
```

### Implementation notes
- **Title mode**: SQL LIKE or fuzzy match on `item.title` + `item.slug`. Fast. Should return in <50ms.
- **Content mode**: Existing FTS5 index (item_fts). Already used by /dash search.
- **Scope filter**:
  - Local: `WHERE owner_id = :user_id`
  - Global: `WHERE visibility IN ('public', 'public_edit', 'unlisted')`
  - User: `WHERE u.username = :username AND visibility IN (...)`
- **Result ordering**: exact title match → title prefix → title substring → content match → recency
- **Permissions**: must re-check visibility for each result — non-owners never see private items

### Edge cases
- Empty query → show recent items or "type to search..."
- No results → show "No results. Try a different query or scope."
- Long query (>500 chars) → truncate
- Rate limit abuse: 50 requests/minute per user

## Why this is important

- **Obsidian users expect Cmd+O/Cmd+K quick switcher** — it's muscle memory for the entire power-user community
- **Agent-first angle**: the same `/api/v1/search/quick` endpoint is also agent-callable, giving agents a fast navigation primitive
- **Replaces the scroll-to-find behavior** in the sidebar tree — much faster for users with 100+ items
- **Matches every modern SaaS** — Linear, Notion, Slack, GitHub, Raycast all have Cmd+K. ListHub not having it makes it feel dated.

## Related tickets

- **listhub-95u** (P1) — Global search + tag filter + sidebar search (this is the broader search ticket; Cmd+K is a focused UI layer on top of it)
- **listhub-16c** — Agent-friendly search surface across all ListHub items (the API side; Cmd+K uses it)
- **listhub-l91** — Tags as first-class feature (tag-mode search eventually depends on this)
- **listhub-46s** — Autocomplete search of existing lists when creating new list (similar UI pattern, different context)

## Prior art / references

- Obsidian Quick Switcher (Cmd+O)
- Linear command palette (Cmd+K)
- Slack Cmd+K channel/DM switcher
- VS Code Cmd+P go-to-file + Cmd+Shift+F full-text search
- Raycast root search

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Cmd+K anywhere on the site opens the overlay
- [ ] Typing fuzzy-matches item titles by default
- [ ] Tab switches scope (Local ↔ Global ↔ Focused)
- [ ] `/content` prefix switches to full-text search
- [ ] Arrow keys + Enter navigate to the selected item
- [ ] Cmd+Enter opens in new tab
- [ ] Escape closes
- [ ] Visibility is respected (no private items leaked to non-owners)
- [ ] Last-used scope persists in localStorage
- [ ] Works on mobile (tap to open, not just Cmd+K)

title: Global search + tag filter + sidebar search
description:
## Goal
A first-class search and filter experience across ListHub that spans your own items, the community directory, and (eventually) other users' public content. The key primitive: a global search bar, with scoped fallbacks.

## Three related features, one ticket (for V1 scoping)

### 1. Global search bar
- Always accessible from the top header (Cmd+K / Ctrl+K keyboard shortcut, per convention from Linear, Notion, GitHub)
- Queries across all visible content: your own items (all visibilities) + public items from everyone + community directory
- Returns grouped results: "Your docs", "Community", "Public (others)"
- Each result = title, path, snippet, visibility badge, click to open
- Server-side: FTS5 with `visibility IN ('public', 'public_edit') OR owner_id = :me` filtering

### 2. Tag filter
- Browse and filter items by tag, surfaced both in the main content area (for list pages) and possibly as a sidebar section or pill row
- Tag autocomplete in the search bar: typing `#` should suggest tags
- Per-page "filter by tag" controls on dashboard, profile, explore, directory
- Tag cloud or tag index page showing all tags and item counts

### 3. Sidebar search / filter
- A small filter input at the top of each sidebar section that fuzzy-filters that section's tree
- Instant feedback (JS-side, no round trip)
- Clearing resets the tree to full
- Independent from the global search; for when you already know you're looking within "Your docs" or "Community"

## Design questions

**Should sidebar search be a filter over the loaded tree (client-side fuzzy) or a live query (server-side)?**
Client-side is simpler for V1 and fast for trees of <500 items. Server-side scales better. Start client-side.

**Should global search be client-side at all, or always server?**
Always server. FTS5 is already wired up via `GET /api/v1/search`, just needs a UI and visibility-scoping fix (currently scoped to owner only, needs a public+owner variant).

**What results look like when there's no match?**
See listhub-3wv (search-or-create fallback) — that's where this pattern extends.

## Scope for V1
1. Global search bar in header (Cmd+K modal)
2. Server endpoint: `GET /api/v1/search/global?q=...&scope=mine|public|all`
3. Sidebar per-section client-side filter input
4. Basic tag filter on the dashboard and explore pages

Defer to V2:
- Tag autocomplete in search
- Tag cloud / index page
- Semantic search (embeddings)
- Cross-user private results (shared-with-me)

## Related
- `listhub-3wv` — Search-or-create fallback (the "ask/create" flow)
- `listhub-16c` — Agent-friendly search (API-side considerations)
- Existing `GET /api/v1/search` — already has keyword search but owner-scoped only

title: Visibility label consistency: rename 'public' display to 'public view', distinct icons, clearer docs
description:
## Problem
2026-04-07 onboarding test: agent thought "public" meant "anyone can edit" and was confused when it could not update a public item. The visibility values "public" and "public_edit" are not visually or semantically distinct enough.

## Decision
Keep the API enum values as `public`, `public_edit`, `private`, `shared`, `unlisted` (do NOT break the API).

CHANGE the display labels and docs to be unambiguous:

| Enum value | Display label | Tooltip |
|---|---|---|
| `public` | **public view** | Anyone can read; only owner can edit |
| `public_edit` | **public edit** | Anyone signed in can read AND edit |
| `private` | private | Only you |
| `shared` | shared | Specific people |
| `unlisted` | unlisted | Public URL but not listed in directories |

## Icon distinction
- `public` → 🌐 (globe)
- `public_edit` → ✏️🌐 or just ✏️ on a green background (writing icon)
- `private` → 🔒
- `shared` → 🔗
- `unlisted` → 👁

Today both `public` and `public_edit` use globe-ish icons that look similar — this is part of the confusion.

## API docs + llms.txt
The /api/docs page must explicitly say:
> **public** = read-only public. Anyone can VIEW. Only the owner can EDIT.
> **public_edit** = wiki-style. Anyone signed in can VIEW and EDIT.

The llms.txt should include this distinction in the visibility section.

## Surfaces to update
- templates/item.html visibility badges
- templates/dash.html filter tabs (currently say "Public" and "Public Edit" — make "Public View" and "Public Edit")
- templates/profile.html, folder.html, _sidebar_tree.html
- API responses (consider adding `visibility_label: "public view"` alongside the enum)
- /api/docs and /llms.txt content

## Related
- 2026-04-07 onboarding test (real user friction)
- listhub-3cm (inline permissions and quick actions)
- listhub-erm (unlisted visibility)

title: (Stretch) Hugging Face dataset companion: jacobcole/llm-wikis
description: Mirror the LLM Wikis index as a HF dataset for permanence. Use ronantakizawa/moltbook format as template. Two-layer persistence (ListHub + HF). This is the actual {topic}book-shaped artifact, even though we're not naming it llmwikibook.

title: Wikis: let users spin up named wikis (extend the /directory model)
description:
## Goal

Let any user spin up their own wiki, in the spirit of the existing /featured (formerly /directory) feature and Jacob's prior wiki sites (rsiwiki.jacobcole.net, capitalsphere.org).

## Conceptual model: Featured vs Wikis

ListHub has two layers of curated knowledge:

1. **Featured** (formerly /directory) — the **canonical layer**. Curated by editors. One official version per topic. Editors *feature* the best/canonical content here. Wikipedia analog. Edit-restricted (long-term).
2. **Wikis** — the **open ecosystem**. Anyone can spin one up. Multiple competing wikis can exist on the same topic. Fandom/Wikia analog. Owner-controlled edit perms.

Example: Featured has the official "list of body masters". Anyone can also create a wiki with their own list of body masters that disagrees. The Featured editors decide which (if any) wiki gets featured as canonical.

## What this ticket adds

The Wikis layer. Specifically: the ability for users to create *named, owner-scoped wikis* that use the same backend pattern as /featured (markdown + git repo + sidebar tree + edit form), but with an owner and a slug.

A user can have one or more wikis. Each wiki is its own bare git repo with its own slug; edit permissions are controlled by the owner.

## Three data-model options

### Option A — One git repo per wiki (matches /featured exactly) ← RECOMMENDED
- New `wiki` table: (id, owner_id, slug, name, description, visibility, edit_policy, repo_path, created_at)
- Each wiki gets its own bare repo at REPO_ROOT/wikis/<owner>-<slug>.git
- Reuse all existing /featured rendering, edit form, and API helpers — just parameterize by repo_path instead of hardcoding to directory.git (or whatever it ends up being called after listhub-bno)
- Pros: clean isolation, follows the /featured model exactly, easy to back up / move / delete a single wiki
- Cons: introduces a real wikis table; needs a migration

### Option B — Subfolders inside one shared wikis git repo
- One repo at REPO_ROOT/wikis.git
- Each wiki = top-level subfolder, e.g. wikis.git/jacobreal/rsi/
- Wiki metadata in a wiki.json or frontmatter on the folder index
- Pros: no per-wiki repo bookkeeping
- Cons: edit perms get tangled — one git history for everyone, harder to back up or transfer ownership

### Option C — Reuse the user item model
- A wiki = flagged folder of items under /@user/file_path
- Lean into the existing per-item visibility primitives
- Pros: nothing new to build on the storage side
- Cons: doesn't match how /featured works at all. /featured is git+markdown, items are SQLite rows. Two different mental models. If wikis are supposed to be in the spirit of /featured, this option is wrong.

**Recommendation: Option A.** Mirrors /featured exactly so the rendering, sidebar, edit form, and API are all reusable. The only new code is wiki creation/management and ownership checks on edit.

## Edit policies

Each wiki picks one at creation:
- **owner_only** — only the owner can edit (like a personal wiki). Default for new wikis.
- **invited** — owner + a list of co-editors
- **public_edit** — anyone signed in can edit (like /featured today)
- **public_open** — anyone, no signin (probably skip in v1)

## URLs

- /wiki/<owner>/<slug>/ — wiki landing (renders index.md)
- /wiki/<owner>/<slug>/<path> — page inside the wiki
- /wiki/<owner>/<slug>/edit/<path> — edit form
- PUT /api/v1/wiki/<owner>/<slug>/<path> — API edit
- POST /api/v1/wiki — create a new wiki (owner = current user)

The existing /featured routes stay as-is. Featured is the canonical layer; user wikis are separate. (Long-term, /featured *could* be folded into the wiki table as a row with owner=null and a special edit_policy, but no need to migrate working code now.)

## Discovery page

New /wikis page that lists user-owned wikis as cards: name, owner, description, page count, last updated. Sortable by recently updated / most pages / newest.

This is **separate from /community → /people** (the user grid) and **separate from /featured** (the curated canonical layer). Three distinct top-level browse pages:

- **/people** — browse users (currently /community, see listhub-a3p)
- **/featured** — the curated canonical layer (currently /directory, see listhub-bno)
- **/wikis** — open ecosystem of user-owned wikis (this ticket)

Cross-link them in the nav:

```
Browse  ·  People  ·  Featured  ·  Wikis  ·  Roadmap
```

## Sidebar treatment on /@user/ profile

Add a Wikis section on /@user/ profile pages above the existing items tree, showing the user's wikis as cards (name, page count, last edited). Each links to /wiki/<owner>/<slug>/.

When viewing a wiki page, the sidebar should show that wiki's tree (with home/index entries — see listhub-dqn for the same pattern needed on /featured).

## Promotion: how Featured features a wiki

The cleanest model: a Featured page can *link to* or *embed* a wiki page as its canonical source. Featured editors decide which wiki gets featured. This means:
- Featured stays small and curated (one entry per topic, with editorial pointer)
- Wikis stay open (anyone can create competing versions)
- The "officialness" lives on the Featured side, not on the wiki side

Implementation: Featured pages can have frontmatter like `featured_wiki: jacobreal/body-masters` that renders an "Official source: [wiki name] by @owner" callout, or embeds the wiki's index.md directly.

## Suggested phasing

- **Phase 1**: wiki table + create-wiki form + per-wiki bare git repo + reuse /featured rendering/edit/API code parameterized by repo_path. Hard-link from /@user/ profile page.
- **Phase 2**: /wikis discovery page listing all public user wikis. Cards, sort options.
- **Phase 3**: edit policies (invited co-editors), wiki settings page, visibility flips, RSS feeds, vanity hostnames (rsi.jacobcole.example pointing at /wiki/jacobreal/rsi/).
- **Phase 4**: Featured-can-promote-wiki integration. Featured pages get a "canonical source" frontmatter field that points at a wiki.

## Connects to

- **listhub-bno**: Rename /directory to /featured. Do this first — once /featured is the canonical name, user wikis become "the other layer" cleanly.
- **listhub-dqn**: Featured needs a home/index entry in the sidebar. Same fix needed for user wikis once they exist.
- **listhub-a3p**: /community → /people rename. Sibling rename effort.
- **listhub-rmb**: Agent-first / programmatic creation — agents should be able to create wikis via POST /api/v1/wiki and write pages via PUT /api/v1/wiki/:owner/:slug/:path
- **listhub-hon**: LLM Wiki Book directory — the /wikis page IS the LLM Wiki directory once user wikis exist
- **listhub-95u**: global search — search needs to honor wiki boundaries (search within a wiki vs across the whole site)
- mockups/ideaflow-memory.html — the company-wiki pitch becomes real once IdeaFlow can spin up its own wiki here
- 2026-04-07 wikis brainstorm: rsiwiki.jacobcole.net + capitalsphere.org as reference designs Jacob has built before

## Things I'm flagging for Jacob's call

- **Should /featured become a wiki row with owner=null?** That would unify everything under one model. Tempting, but a migration. Probably defer to phase 4.
- **Per-wiki vs per-page edit permissions.** /featured today is wiki-wide public_edit. Do we want per-page locks? Probably not in v1.
- **Naming.** The model is "wiki". The brand for the company-wiki pitch is "IdeaFlow Memory". Pick the public-facing word now to avoid renaming later. Lean toward "Wiki" because users already use rsiwiki.jacobcole.net etc.
- **Repo storage.** REPO_ROOT/wikis/<owner>-<slug>.git could blow up — disk quotas? Probably fine for v1 since markdown is tiny.

title: Download user git repo as ZIP — full for owner, filtered for others
description:
## Goal
Add a "Download as ZIP" button on user profile pages and the dashboard that bundles up the user's git repo as a downloadable archive.

## Behavior

### When the viewer IS the owner
- Downloads the **full** bare git repo (or a snapshot of all items as markdown files)
- Includes private + shared + public + public_edit
- Preserves the folder structure (file_path)
- Includes YAML frontmatter on each file (title, slug, visibility, type, tags)
- Includes `.folder.yaml` files when listhub-74j ships

### When the viewer is NOT the owner
- Downloads a **filtered** view: only items with visibility in (`public`, `public_edit`, `unlisted`)
- Same structure preservation
- Could be served live (no caching) since the filter is dynamic per requestor

## Implementation sketch

1. **Server-side ZIP generation**: walk the user's items, write each as a markdown file with frontmatter to an in-memory ZIP, return as `application/zip`
2. **Endpoint**: `GET /api/v1/users/:username/download.zip` (no auth = filtered, with auth as owner = full)
3. **UI**: "Download as ZIP" button on `/@username` profile page next to the Home button. Possibly also a "Download my repo" button on `/dash`.
4. **Filename**: `{username}-listhub-{date}.zip`
5. **Filter logic**: same visibility check used elsewhere (`visibility IN ('public', 'public_edit', 'unlisted')`)

## Why
- Lets users grab their own data offline (data portability is a core ListHub principle)
- Lets non-owners take a snapshot of someone's public content for their own archive (like cloning a public GitHub repo)
- Complements the existing `git clone` access via the bare repo HTTP endpoint, which is more developer-shaped
- ZIP is the universal "save this for me" format — works for non-developers

## Related
- listhub-ev1 — Clone URL and download button (parent feature, still open)
- listhub-xtc — GitHub-like repo UX epic
- listhub-74j — Folder-level visibility (the filter has to walk this tree once it ships)
- listhub-r62 — Agent-native Drive replacement epic
- The git_sync.py module already generates per-item markdown with frontmatter — reuse that logic for the ZIP build

## Edge cases
- Very large repos (100MB+): stream the response, don't build in memory. Use Python's `zipfile` with a streaming wrapper.
- Empty repos: return a zip with just a README explaining the repo is empty
- Private + non-existent users: 404
- Concurrent downloads: cache the filtered ZIP for ~5 minutes by (user, viewer_visibility_set) hash to avoid rebuilding

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `GET /@username/download.zip` returns a ZIP with filtered or full content
- [ ] Owner sees "Download my full repo" button on /@username and /dash
- [ ] Non-owner sees "Download public items" button on /@username
- [ ] ZIP file uses canonical YAML frontmatter format
- [ ] Folder structure is preserved
- [ ] Tested with @jacobreal (90+ items, mixed visibility)

title: Directory/showcase view template (like renaissance.engineer, deep-dialogues)
description:
Create a view template/theme system so ListHub items and collections can be displayed in rich, curated layouts — beyond the current simple markdown rendering.

Inspiration:
- renaissance.engineer: people directory with expandable profile cards, discipline tags/badges, vision statements, project links, stats
- deep-dialogues.ideaflow.app: conversation/dialogue display with speaker labels, threaded responses

Both are essentially curated lists with rich rendering.

Design options:
1. Template system: items can specify a display template (directory, showcase, dialogue, cards)
2. Custom CSS per item/collection
3. Structured data in frontmatter that templates consume

Key insight: ListHub already has the data layer (items, tags, markdown). What's missing is the presentation layer — templates that turn a list of items into a visually compelling page.

Template types to support:
- Directory (cards with metadata, filtering, search)
- Showcase (featured items with rich media)
- Dialogue (conversation format with speaker labels)
- Cards (grid layout with thumbnails)

Related: listhub-ogp (directory UI specifically), listhub-00k (flowtools as first directory content)

title: Rename /directory to /featured (route, sidebar label, all references)
description:
## What

Rename the existing /directory feature to /featured everywhere. The current state is inconsistent: the sidebar section is labeled "Community", the route is /directory, the action button tooltip says "Open directory", and the underlying variable is sidebar_community_tree. Three different words for the same thing.

The new name is **Featured**. Featured because the long-term concept is that the page is a curated/canonical layer where editors **feature** the official version of each topic (the official body masters list, the official superconnectors list, etc.). Wikipedia-style canonical layer on top of an open ecosystem of competing user wikis (see listhub-a2o).

## Concrete changes

### Routes (views.py)
- def directory(subpath=) → def featured(subpath=)
- @views_bp.route(/directory) → @views_bp.route(/featured)
- @views_bp.route(/directory/<path:subpath>) → @views_bp.route(/featured/<path:subpath>)
- def directory_edit → def featured_edit
- @views_bp.route(/directory/edit) → @views_bp.route(/featured/edit)
- Add 301 redirect: /directory → /featured (and /directory/<path> → /featured/<path>) for backward compat
- _directory_repo() → keep helper name (its an internal helper) but path can stay directory.git on disk so we dont have to move git repos. Or rename to _featured_repo and rename the bare repo. **Recommendation: rename helper but keep repo path on disk.**
- _sidebar_community_tree → _sidebar_featured_tree
- _get_directory_topics → _get_featured_topics

### API (api.py)
- /api/v1/directory/:path → /api/v1/featured/:path
- _directory_repo_path → _featured_repo_path (helper rename)
- _dir_commit_file → _featured_commit_file
- Add 301/308 redirects from /api/v1/directory/* → /api/v1/featured/*

### Sidebar (templates/_sidebar.html)
- Section header label: "Community" → "Featured"
- aria-label "Toggle Community" → "Toggle Featured"
- data-sb-section="community" → data-sb-section="featured"
- url_for(views.directory) → url_for(views.featured) (×2)
- title="Open directory" → title="Open featured"
- link_prefix /directory/ → /featured/
- storage_key sb_community → sb_featured (note: this changes the localStorage key, so users will lose their collapse state once)
- {% if sidebar_community_tree %} → {% if sidebar_featured_tree %}
- "Community directory is empty." → "Featured page is empty."

### Context processor (views.py)
- sidebar_community_tree key → sidebar_featured_tree
- _sidebar_community_tree() function rename

### Other templates
- templates/community.html — only references community in the URL switcher, not the directory. **Leave alone**, this is the user-grid page.
- templates/api_docs.html — update any references to /api/v1/directory
- templates/landing.html — check for hardcoded /directory links
- templates/browse.html — directory_topics → featured_topics
- templates/directory.html → templates/featured.html (rename file)
- All other hardcoded /directory references in markdown content (llms.txt, README, mockups, etc.)

### llms.txt / sitemap / RSS
- Update any hardcoded /directory URLs to /featured
- llms.txt has a list of routes — update it

### CSS
- .sb-section[data-sb-section=community] → [data-sb-section=featured] if any styles are scoped this way

### Cache buster
- Bump sidebar.css?v=10-perf → v=11-featured (sidebar markup changed)

## Out of scope (separate tickets)
- Adding a home/index entry to the Featured sidebar tree (currently index.md is filtered out — see listhub-dqn)
- Tightening edit perms on /featured (currently anyone-signed-in can edit, which may be too loose for a canonical layer — defer until canonical-layer semantics are clearer)

## Connects to
- listhub-a2o: Wikis feature — Featured is the canonical/curated counterpart to the open Wikis ecosystem
- listhub-dqn: Featured needs a home/index page entry in the sidebar
- listhub-a3p: /community → /people rename (sibling rename effort, similar pattern)

## Why "Featured" specifically
Jacob picked Featured over Selected, Canon, Atlas, Index, The Directory. "Featured" is the most self-explanatory of the candidates and matches the intended editorial mode (curators **feature** the canonical version of each topic). No mystery, no jargon, no rebrand needed.

Admin/editor mechanism to curate what appears on the ListHub landing page (/).

title: API key recovery: feature POST /auth/token as the canonical 'lost my key' path + document prominently
description:
## Problem
2026-04-07 onboarding test: agent registered via POST /api/v1/auth/register, got an API key shown ONCE in the response, then lost access to it. There is currently no way to retrieve or regenerate a key without having a logged-in session — but the agent has no Noos identity and no clear path to logging in.

## Solution exists but is undocumented
**The endpoint already exists**: POST /api/v1/auth/token accepts username + password and returns a new API key. It is documented in /api/docs but not as the canonical "I lost my key" recovery path.

ACTION: rename, document, and prominently feature this endpoint as the "API key recovery" path so agents (and humans) know how to recover from a lost key.

## Required changes

1. **api.py**: rename or alias /auth/token → /auth/key (or add a new /auth/recover-key endpoint that is identical). Keep /auth/token as backwards-compatible alias.

2. **/api/docs**: add a prominent "Lost your API key?" section near the top of the auth docs that calls out POST /auth/token as the recovery path. Make this section findable via section anchor + ToC link.

3. **/llms.txt**: add to the agent quick-start section:
   ```
   ## Lost your API key
   POST /api/v1/auth/token
   {"username": "...", "password": "...", "name": "recovery-1"}
   Returns: {"key": "mem_..."}
   ```

4. **Settings page**: when a user is signed in, the Settings page should show a "Generate new API key" button that calls /auth/token (with the current session) and returns the new key.

5. **Registration response**: include a note in the response saying "If you lose this key, get a new one via POST /api/v1/auth/token with your credentials."

## Pairs well with listhub-r7u (API-key login)
Once API-key login exists (paste-your-key web auth), the recovery flow becomes:
1. Agent registers, gets key, loses it
2. Agent calls POST /auth/token with username+password → gets new key
3. Agent (or owner) pastes key at /login/key → has a browser session
4. Owner can now manage the agent account via UI

## Related
- listhub-rmb (Noos auth migration)
- listhub-r7u (API-key login)
- 2026-04-07 onboarding test

title: Inline image support for markdown items (Obsidian-style ![[image.png]])
description: ## Goal Support inline images in markdown items, at minimum matching what Obsidian does. ## Current state ListHub items are markdown-only. Images can be referenced via URL (`![](https://...)`) but: - No upload pipeline - No inline image storage alongside items - No Obsidian-style wikilink image embeds (`![[screenshot.png]]`) ## Proposed scope — v1 (Obsidian parity) ### Upload + storage - `POST /api/v1/files` (multipart) → returns `{url, hash, size, mime_type}` - Store binary content-addressed (sha256) in a blob store - Initial blob store: local disk under `LISTHUB_BLOB_ROOT` (simple, migrate later) - Files are owned — attached to a user via `file_owner` or similar - Max upload size: 25 MB initially - Allowed types: image/png, image/jpeg, image/webp, image/gif, image/svg+xml (no executable SVG) ### Markdown rendering - Support standard markdown `![alt](url)` — already works for remote URLs - Support local refs `![alt](/files/.png)` - Support Obsidian-style `![[screenshot.png]]` that resolves to the owner attachment - Render as `` with lazy loading, cap width with CSS ### Visibility inheritance - Files inherit the visibility of the item that references them - If an item is public, its images are fetchable publicly - If an item is private, the image URL requires auth (signed URL or session check) ### Editor UX (v1) - Drag/drop onto the Milkdown editor → upload → insert markdown ref - Paste image from clipboard → same flow ## Obsidian parity checklist - [x] ![alt](url) syntax - [ ] ![[image.png]] wikilink syntax - [ ] Drag-drop upload in editor - [ ] Clipboard paste upload - [ ] Image preview in edit mode - [ ] Image resizing via CSS / markdown width attr ## Nice-to-haves (future) - Image alt text inference via vision model on upload - Thumbnail generation for image grids - EXIF stripping for privacy - Content-addressed deduplication (same image referenced from multiple items shares one blob) ## Related - listhub-2zv — file bucket / upload architecture (foundational decision for THIS ticket) - listhub-4jm — multi-sheet CSV support (adjacent non-markdown format) - listhub-2zb — auto-push to GitHub (images need to be pushed too)

title: Add graph database support for item relationships/links
Audit sidebar tree expand/collapse design and behavior
description:
## Audit findings (2026-04-07)

Two tree components exist and have DIFFERENT behaviors:

### `templates/tree.html` + `static/tree.js` (used by dash.html, profile.html)
- **Toggle**: entire folder row is clickable to toggle collapse. `tree.js`
  `initTree()` attaches a click listener to `.tree-row--folder`.
- **Nested collapse awareness**: expand walks descendants and respects the
  collapsed state of subfolders (smart — good).
- **Link protection**: `if (e.target.closest(a)) return;` prevents toggle
  when clicking a link inside the row.
- **Chevron**: was decorative only (rotated via CSS on `.collapsed` class).
  **Partially fixed in commit cc768f3**: chevron now has explicit click
  handler with `stopPropagation + preventDefault` and `cursor: pointer`.

### `templates/_sidebar_tree.html` (used by _sidebar.html)
- **Toggle**: explicit chevron button with `aria-expanded` attribute.
- **Folder link**: folder name is a separate `<a class="sb-folder-link">`
  that navigates to the folder view.
- **Different from tree.html**: this one already had the right separation
  of concerns.

## Remaining issues

- [ ] **No persistent state**: refresh loses collapse state. Neither tree
  persists via localStorage. Should persist keyed on `(owner, view)`.
- [ ] **No default-expand behavior**: all folders start expanded. Nice to
  have: auto-expand the folder containing the current item when viewing
  an item page from the sidebar.
- [ ] **Empty folder placeholder rendering**: tree.html shows `<div
  class="tree-row--empty">` with "Empty" text for folders with 0 files.
  Good feature but worth checking if it looks right with the new folder
  drill-down link.
- [ ] **Two separate tree implementations**: `tree.html` vs
  `_sidebar_tree.html`. Consider consolidating. They have inconsistent
  class names (`tree-*` vs `sb-*`), styling, and behavior. File a followup
  ticket for consolidation.
- [ ] **Keyboard navigation**: neither tree supports arrow-key nav.
  Should file followup ticket.
- [ ] **aria-expanded**: only _sidebar_tree.html sets aria-expanded. tree.html
  does not, so it is less screen-reader friendly.

## Status
Partial fix landed (kdg + chevron click) in cc768f3. The persistent state,
default-expand, and tree consolidation are follow-up work. This ticket stays
open with a checklist above; spawn child tickets as you decide to tackle each.

title: Profile page split layout: home item content + project/folder list together
description:
## Goal
When visiting /@username, show BOTH the user's home item content AND their list of projects/folders/lists in a unified layout. Earlier Claude session captured this as an open question on listhub-ftr; today's user feedback resolves it as "show both."

## Current behavior
- /@username renders profile.html
- profile.html shows the folder/list view of the user's items
- If the user has a `home` item, a small button appears in the header linking to it
- The home item content is NOT rendered on the profile page itself

## Proposed split layout

```
+-------------------------------------------------+
| @username                          [🏠 Home]    |
| Display Name                                    |
+-------------------------------------------------+
|                                                 |
|  HOME ITEM CONTENT (if present)                 |
|  Welcome message, curated links, intro...       |
|  (Rendered markdown from the `home` item)       |
|                                                 |
+-------------------------------------------------+
|                                                 |
|  PROJECTS / FOLDERS / LISTS                     |
|  📁 chronicpain (5 items)                       |
|  📁 foodslist (4 items)                         |
|  📁 ideaflow (3 items)                          |
|  📄 home                                        |
|  📄 personal-index                              |
|  ...                                            |
|                                                 |
+-------------------------------------------------+
```

## Behaviors
- If user has NO home item: show only the project list (current behavior)
- If user HAS a home item: render its content at the top, then show the project list below
- The home item is NOT also listed in the projects list (avoid duplication)
- Owners see a small "Edit home page" link next to the home content
- Visitors see clean rendered content without any edit affordances

## Implementation
- views.py user_profile: also fetch home_item content (already fetches home_item metadata for the badge — extend to fetch content)
- profile.html: add a section above the folder tree that renders home_item content if present
- Use the same render_md(is_owner=...) helper so private blocks are stripped for non-owners
- Style the home content section with a subtle background to distinguish it from the project list

## Open questions
- Should the home content be collapsible? (Yes, with localStorage-remembered state)
- Should non-public home items show on someone else's profile? (No — visibility check, same as items)
- Should there be a default home content if the user has not created one? (No, just hide the section)

## Related
- listhub-ftr (central home doc) — this is the "show both" answer to the open question
- listhub-r62.1 (metadata pattern)
- 2026-04-07 user feedback

title: Featured page (formerly /directory) needs a home/index entry in the sidebar
description:
## Problem

Every user tree has a special **home** entry hoisted to the top — clicking it goes to /@user/ and renders that user's home item. The Featured tree (formerly Community/directory) does not have an equivalent.

Specifically: in views.py the `_sidebar_community_tree` (renamed to `_sidebar_featured_tree` per listhub-bno) `to_nodes` function explicitly **drops** index.md from the rendered list. The relevant line:

    leaf_files = sorted_files_minus_index_md  # see views.py: filter "if f != 'index.md'"

This means:
- The root index.md (the landing page for /featured) is invisible from the sidebar — you can't click into it from the tree, only the Featured section header itself navigates there.
- Nested folders that have an index.md (their own landing page) lose the ability to surface it as a clickable home entry inside the sidebar.

## What it should do

Match the user-tree behavior:

1. **Root**: hoist index.md to the top of the Featured tree as a special **home** entry. Name it "Home" (or use the title from frontmatter). Clicking goes to /featured (the route already renders the root index.md).
2. **Nested folders**: each folder that contains an index.md should also surface a home entry at the top of its children — or render the folder name itself as a clickable link to its index page. Clicking goes to /featured/<folder-path>/.

## Why this matters

- **Discoverability**: index pages are the curated landing copy for each topic. Right now they only render when you navigate to the route directly — invisible from the sidebar tree.
- **Consistency**: user trees and the Featured tree should behave the same way.
- **Forward-compat**: once the Wikis feature ships (listhub-a2o), each user wiki will need the same thing — a home entry per wiki and per nested folder. Building it once for Featured gives us the pattern for free.

## Implementation hint

User trees solve this via the `home_files` / `other_files` split inside `_sidebar_build_user_tree_from_items` plus a `file_node(f, is_home=True)` call that flags the entry for special rendering. The Featured tree should mirror that pattern: detect index.md per folder, mark it as the folder's home entry, and render it differently in the template (probably as a small house icon + folder name, or just "Home" at the top).

## Connects to

- **listhub-bno**: rename /directory to /featured (do that first or at the same time)
- **listhub-a2o**: Wikis feature (the same fix will be needed once user wikis exist)

title: Document converter: .docx / .doc / .rtf → markdown on import
description:
## Goal
Convert uploaded .docx, .doc, and .rtf files to markdown at import time, creating a ListHub item from each.

## Approach
- Use pandoc via subprocess for the conversion: `pandoc -f docx -t gfm -o out.md input.docx`
- Preserve headings, lists, tables, bold/italic, links
- Images get uploaded as attachments (requires listhub-c6x + listhub-2zv)
- First pass can skip images; inline image support comes later

## Scope
- **Trigger**: user drops a .docx file into the upload zone
- **Server-side**: pandoc converts to markdown, the result becomes an item
- **Frontmatter**: title derived from first heading or filename, visibility private, type document
- **Error handling**: if pandoc fails, return the error to the user; keep the uploaded file in a temp location for retry

## Tech notes
- pandoc is already a Ubuntu apt package — `apt install pandoc` on prod
- Alternative: python-docx library for .docx only, but pandoc handles more formats in one tool
- Conversion quality for complex docs (nested tables, styled content) will be imperfect — thats fine, markdown is lossier than docx by design

## Related
- listhub-r62 — epic
- listhub-ma7 — markdown upload (prerequisite for the upload pipeline)
- listhub-c6x — image support needed for image-containing docs

title: Add unlisted visibility option
description: 4th visibility level: accessible via direct URL but not in directory/explore/profile. Like YouTube unlisted.

title: Clone URL and download button for repos/lists
description:
## Goal
Add a prominent clone URL and download button to user profile/repo pages, giving the GitHub "green Code button" feel.

## Features
1. **Clone URL display**: Show a copyable `git clone listhub.globalbr.ai/@username.git` URL on each user's public page
2. **Download as ZIP**: Button to download a snapshot of a user's public items as a .zip or .tar.gz
3. **Generated public repo**: Even if the actual bare repo contains private items, generate a filtered clone-friendly version with only public content
4. **Community directory**: The community repo should be especially easy to clone — featured on the explore page

## Implementation Notes
- ListHub already has bare git repos per user — the clone URL could serve these directly via git-http-backend or smart HTTP protocol
- For privacy: need a git filter that strips private items before serving (or maintain a separate public-only bare repo that's updated on each commit)
- Download ZIP is simpler: just archive the public items as markdown files in a folder structure
- Consider showing the clone command in a modal/dropdown like GitHub does

## UX Reference
GitHub's "Code" button shows:
- HTTPS clone URL (copyable)
- SSH clone URL
- GitHub CLI command
- "Download ZIP" link
- "Open with GitHub Desktop"

We don't need all of these — start with HTTPS clone URL + Download ZIP.

title: Public/private split for user home page (and per-line visibility)
description:
## Goal
Consider alternatives to the current approach for mixing public and private content in the user home page.

## Shipped implementation (2026-04-07)

**Option A + Option C** (combined):
1. **Option A: Two separate pages** — `/@username/home` is public, `/dash` is private dashboard owner-only. Cross-linked via Home buttons.
2. **Option C: HTML-comment private blocks** — any item can contain `<!-- private -->...<!-- /private -->` blocks that are stripped at render time for non-owners. Works across ALL items, not just home. Documented in `/llms.txt` and `/api/docs`. Implemented in `views.py:render_md(is_owner=)` and `strip_private_blocks()`. Also filters the copy-to-clipboard textarea.

See commit: 771e359 and 92920aa.

## Options NOT yet shipped (for future consideration)

### Option B: Per-line visibility markers (markdown extension)
Instead of block-level comments, support per-line markers like:
```
- Public item
- [private] Secret item only I see
- Another public item
```
Pros: finer granularity, more natural.
Cons: needs a real markdown extension, editor UX, and an agent contract.

### Option D: Encrypted per-section (age or libsodium sealed boxes)
Server cannot see the content at all. Owner decrypts client-side.
Pros: truly private — content is protected even from DB access.
Cons: key management complexity, agent-hostile (agents cant read what they cant decrypt), requires frontend crypto.

### Option E: content + content_private fields on the item schema
Separate database columns for public vs private. Cleaner than inline markers, ugly for narrative flow.

### Option F: Dashboard composes home
`/dash` renders the home page content at the top + additional private sections below. Single source for home, plus extras.

## Decision criteria for revisit

Consider revisiting if:
- Users find `<!-- private -->` too clunky for frequent use
- A common pattern emerges where per-line privacy would be better than per-block
- Encryption becomes necessary for compliance or trust reasons
- The copy-to-clipboard / API-read surface keeps leaking private content in unexpected ways

## Related
- listhub-ftr — home page feature (done)
- Commits: 92920aa (feat: private content blocks), 771e359 (fix: textarea leak)

title: Central user dashboard document / home index for each account
description:
## Problem
Each user currently has no single "home base" document on their account.
When a user signs up, their dashboard is a generic item list. There is no
central index or landing page for their own content that they can shape.

## Proposal
Give every user a special dashboard/index item that:
- Is auto-created at registration (or on first access)
- Lives at /@username (or /@username/home or /@username/index)
- Is the user-controlled landing page for their repo
- Is a regular ListHub item, editable as markdown, so the user can
  customize it however they want (curated links, intro text, featured posts)
- Gets surfaced as the default view when someone visits /@username
- Can optionally be pinned in the sidebar tree so it is always one click away

## Questions
- Default content: empty? seeded with a template listing their recent items?
- If the user already has a lot of items, should the default just be the
  existing folder-tree view? (Current behavior)
- Relationship to the "Personal Index" item I just created
  (/@jacobreal/personal-index) — should that become the canonical home doc?
- Is this the same as a pinned sidebar item? See also listhub-lav (sidebar ticket).

## Related
- The new /@jacobreal/personal-index item — real-world test case
- listhub-lav — persistent sidebar
- listhub-lwh — sub-wiki concept (similar but folder-scoped)

title: Implement community directory — shared git repo at /directory

No DB storage — rendered directly from git. title: Community wiki as curated index: awesome-list-of-awesome-lists model for /featured
description:
## The insight

The community wiki (/featured) should be an **index that points to the best user content across ListHub** — not a wiki that contains its own content. It is an awesome-list-of-awesome-lists.

## User stories

1. @jacobreal creates a great body masters list at /@jacobreal/body-masters
2. An editor adds it to the community wiki index: "Best body masters list → /@jacobreal/body-masters"
3. @farzapedia creates an ML concepts wiki
4. It gets featured: "ML fundamentals → /@farzapedia/ml-concepts"
5. A visitor lands on /featured and sees a curated directory of the best content on the platform, organized by topic — every link goes to real user content

## What this means for /featured

The community wiki is NOT a place where editors write original content. It is a **curation layer** — an editorial index that says "for topic X, the best resource is this user's list/wiki/folder." The value-add is the curation judgment, not the content itself.

This is like:
- awesome-list pointing to other awesome-lists
- Wikipedia's "List of lists of lists"
- A library catalog (metadata + pointers, not the books themselves)
- HN/Reddit front page (curated links to other people's content)

## v1: literally one index page

The simplest version is a single curated markdown page at /featured/index.md:

```markdown
# Featured on ListHub

## Health & Wellness
- [Body Masters List](/@jacobreal/body-masters) — comprehensive directory of body masters by @jacobreal
- [Chronic Pain Research](/@jacobreal/chronicpain/) — research collection by @jacobreal

## AI & Technology  
- [Agent-Friendly Tools](/@startxwiki/agent-tools) — curated by @startxwiki
- [LLM Wiki Index](/@farzapedia/llm-wiki) — ML concepts by @farzapedia

## Education
- [AdmitSphere](/@jacobreal/admitsphere/) — college admissions wiki
```

Each link is a pointer to real user content. The featured wiki is the table of contents for the whole platform.

## Why this is better than containing content

- **No duplication**: the content lives with its author, not copied into /featured
- **Credit flows to creators**: visitors land on /@username pages, not /featured pages
- **Low maintenance**: editors just curate links, not maintain parallel content
- **Incentive alignment**: users are motivated to make great content because getting featured is visible social proof
- **Scales naturally**: more users → more candidate content → richer index

## Open questions

1. Should the index be one flat page, or organized as topic sub-pages (/featured/health, /featured/ai)?
2. Can users self-nominate ("feature my list") or is it editor-only?
3. Should featured entries show live metadata (item count, last updated) pulled from the linked content?
4. Does featuring create a formal relationship (featured_in table) or is it just a markdown link?

## Relationship to other tickets

- listhub-bno — the /featured route rename (prereq, almost done)
- listhub-bqe — homepage curation mechanism (this ticket clarifies WHAT gets curated)
- listhub-a2o — wikis as objects (featured entries would point to wikis once they exist)
- listhub-dlo — profile split (featured people entries show home content preview)
- listhub-bd1 — showcase view template (the visual rendering of the index)

title: LLM Wiki Book — curated index of LLM wikis on ListHub

First-mover opportunity — Karpathy's gist is days old (2026-04-03), no directory spans all 5 senses, no Moltbook-style archive exists. Full research + plan: research/llm-wiki-research.md and ListHub item /@researchagent/llm-wiki-book-on-listhub-research-plan. List title: 'LLM Wikis' (avoid 'llmwikibook' — reads derivative). Structure: sections by sense, each entry has sense/type/maintainer/status/url/description fields. Ship within 2 weeks while term is fresh.

title: Folder drill-down: clicking a folder opens the zoomed-in folder page
description:
## Problem
On views that show items organized by folder (my lists / dashboard / profile
folder view), clicking a folder header should open a zoomed-in folder page
that shows only the items in that folder with breadcrumbs and a clean layout.
Right now the expand/collapse in the tree may work but there is no
drill-down destination.

## Current state
- views.py has a user_folder route at line 267 that renders templates/folder.html
- Unclear whether the folder tree rows actually link to that route correctly
- Needs an audit of every folder rendering in the app (dash.html, profile.html,
  explore.html, browse.html, _sidebar_tree.html)

## Acceptance criteria
- Clicking a folder row in the sidebar opens /@user/folder-path or /dash?folder=...
- Clicking a folder row in the dashboard also drills down
- The zoomed-in folder page shows breadcrumbs, the folder name, and all items
- Back-navigation works

## Related

- listhub-t8h (nav consistency audit — this is one of the items)

title: Tags as a first-class feature: cross-cutting organization
description:
## Goal
Make tags a first-class organizing primitive in ListHub, alongside folders and visibility.

## Current state
- Items have tags (stored in item_tag join table)
- Tags are displayed as small chips on item rows and item pages
- Tags are passed in the API on create/update
- BUT: there is no /tags page, no tag-clouds, no tag autocomplete in the editor, no rename, no merge, no synonyms

## Why "first-class"
Folders are hierarchical (one item lives in one folder). Tags are cross-cutting (one item has many tags, tags span folders). Both have value:
- Folders give shape and navigation
- Tags give discoverability and grouping by topic

We should make tags work as a parallel organization scheme, not a forgotten attribute.

## Proposed feature scope

### View
- /@user/tags - list of all tags the user has, sorted by item count
- /@user/tags/<tag> - all items with that tag (filtered to viewable visibility)
- /tags/<tag> - all PUBLIC items across the whole platform with that tag (cross-user discovery)
- Tag chips are clickable everywhere they appear

### Manage
- Tag rename: change "python" to "python3" once, applies everywhere
- Tag merge: combine "js", "javascript", "JS" into one
- Tag delete: remove a tag from all items
- Tag aliases / synonyms

### Editor UX
- Tag autocomplete in the new/edit form (suggest existing tags)
- Suggested tags based on content (use embeddings or simple keyword match)
- Quick-add chips below the title field

### Discovery
- Tag cloud on the explore page
- Related tags - tags that frequently co-occur
- Tag-as-navigation in the sidebar (alongside folders)

### Sidebar
- Maybe a "Tags" section in the sidebar (like Your docs / Community / Focused)
- Shows the user top N tags as clickable chips

## Open questions
- Should tags be PER-USER or GLOBAL?
- Should tags have hierarchies (lang slash python)? Or stay flat?
- Auto-tag from content via NLP? Probably future work.

## Related
- listhub-zyc - Comments / suggested additions
- listhub-74j - Folder visibility (parallel organization scheme)
- listhub-r62 - Drive replacement epic

title: Three-section persistent sidebar (Your docs / Community / Focused @user)
description:
## Goal
Add a persistent three-section left-hand sidebar to every page of ListHub, inspired by Google Sites / Notion / Workflowy / Obsidian navigation. Matches Jacob's mental model of how a personal+community knowledge workspace should work.

## The three sections (all collapsible, all always accessible)

### Section 1: Your docs
- Tree of the current user's items organized by `file_path`
- Collapsible folders, visibility badges per item (🔒 / 🌐 / ✏️)
- Click a file → opens in the main pane
- "+ New" button at the top
- Scoped filter/search input
- Hover/right-click actions: rename, move, duplicate, visibility toggle, delete
- Keyboard nav (arrow keys, Enter, cmd-shift-n for new)
- Collapse state persisted to localStorage keyed on folder path

### Section 2: Community directory
- Tree of the shared `directory.git` community repo
- Same tree component as section 1, read-only rendering
- "Contribute" action for authenticated users
- This is the commons — every user sees the same tree
- Backed by `_directory_repo()` code that already exists in `views.py`

### Section 3: Focused on @username
- Contextual — only appears when viewing another user's repo/item
- Shows their public items as a tree
- **Auto-focus**: viewing `/@username/...` makes them the current focus
- **Manual pin**: "📌 Follow" button makes them stay even when you navigate away
- **Multiple pins**: section 3 becomes "Following" with multiple sub-trees
- Stored in a new `user_follow` table

## Implementation plan

1. **Consolidate tree rendering**: the tree component is already implemented three times in `dash.html`, `profile.html`, and `directory.html`. Extract to a single Jinja include (`_tree.html`) taking a data structure parameter.

2. **Add sidebar to `base.html`**: wrap main content in a layout: `[header] → [sidebar | main content] → [footer]`. Sidebar is a `<aside>` with three `<details>` elements for collapsibility.

3. **Data fetching**:
   - Your tree → cached in session, invalidated on create/update/delete
   - Community tree → cached heavily (git HEAD doesn't change often)
   - Focused tree → fetched when URL matches `/@username/...`

4. **Keyboard + localStorage**: small vanilla JS for collapse state and keyboard navigation

5. **Mobile**: sidebar becomes a slide-out drawer below some breakpoint (cmd-\ to toggle on desktop too)

6. **Don't break existing navigation**: sidebar AUGMENTS the existing header/profile/explore navigation for V1. Later we'll prune the older nav where it duplicates.

## Data model addition

```sql
CREATE TABLE user_follow (
    user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES user(id),
    follows_user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES user(id),
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (user_id, follows_user_id)
);
```

## Related
- Checkpoint video (pre-sidebar): https://www.loom.com/share/90b46940568a459480e3e00384907749
- `docs/history-pre-sidebar.md` — context doc
- `listhub-lwh` — sub-wiki concept that builds on this
- `listhub-2lg` — git history viewer (complementary)
- `listhub-60j` — consolidate edit experiences (will benefit from the consolidated tree component)

## Inspiration
- Google Sites: admitsphere.org, rsiwiki.jacobcole.net — both use the exact 3-section mental model (site nav, pages, other sites)
- Wikispaces (ex-admitsphere) — the sidebar tree was core to the wiki experience
- Notion / Workflowy / Obsidian — modern reference implementations

title: Sub-wiki concept: host legacy Google Sites content on ListHub
description:
## Goal
Allow ListHub to host wiki-style content collections as "sub-wikis" within a user's namespace, replacing older wiki platforms like Google Sites (currently hosting admitsphere.org and rsiwiki.jacobcole.net) and legacy Wikispaces.

## Vision
A sub-wiki is just a folder at the top of a user's item tree that happens to be (mostly) public and has a thoughtful `index.md` that acts as a landing page.

Examples:
- `/@jacobreal/admitsphere/` — college admissions resource (currently on Google Sites, originally Wikispaces)
- `/@jacobreal/rsi-wiki/` — RSI recovery resource (currently on Google Sites)
- `/@jacobreal/body-masters/` — could expand from a single list into a sub-wiki with per-practitioner pages

## V1: no special features needed
For the initial sidebar launch, a sub-wiki is literally just:
- A folder with `visibility: public`
- An `index.md` inside it that acts as the landing page
- The sidebar renders it as an expandable folder like any other

No schema changes, no special UI. You just publish a folder and tell people about it.

## V2: affordances that make sub-wikis feel like sub-wikis
- Per-folder landing page (auto-render `index.md` when visiting `/@user/folder/`)
- Per-folder custom title/description/icon
- Optional per-folder custom URL subdomain (e.g., `admitsphere.jacobcole.listhub.globalbr.ai`)
- Per-folder navigation view: a sub-wiki has its own internal left nav (the subset of the sidebar scoped to that folder)
- Per-folder visibility inheritance: "make everything under this folder public unless marked otherwise"

## V3: import tooling
- Google Sites exporter → ListHub importer (walk the Google Sites page tree, create items preserving hierarchy)
- Wikispaces export support (unlikely to matter in 2026 but the export format is documented)
- Notion export → ListHub importer
- Obsidian vault → ListHub (already possible via the REST API)

## Related
- `listhub-lav` — the sidebar that makes sub-wikis navigable
- `listhub-2zb` — encrypted items architecture (sub-wikis with mixed public/private content)
- `listhub-xtc` epic — GitHub-like repo UX
- `docs/history-pre-sidebar.md` — context on current Google Sites usage

## Open questions
- Custom subdomain per sub-wiki: worth it for branding (admitsphere.org style)? Requires DNS wildcard.
- How does the main community directory relate to a user's sub-wikis? Are they cross-linked by default?
- Editing permissions: can a sub-wiki be collaboratively edited (`public_edit` on the whole folder)?

title: Markdown file upload — drag-drop .md files to create items
description:
## Goal
Let users drag-drop .md files into ListHub to create new items, parsing existing frontmatter if present.

## Scope
- Single-file drop zone on /dash (or a new /dash/upload route)
- Reads the file as text
- If it has YAML frontmatter: use the title/slug/visibility/tags/type from there
- If not: derive title from first heading or filename, default visibility to private, type to document
- Creates an item via the existing `POST /api/v1/items/new` path
- Shows a toast on success with a link to the new item

## Why this first
Smallest unit of file upload — no binary storage needed, no mime-type handling, no format conversion. Pure text → item. Proves the drop-zone interaction works before we extend to images (listhub-c6x) and arbitrary files (listhub-2zv).

## Related
- listhub-r62 — epic
- listhub-67v — bulk folder upload is the next step up
- listhub-c6x — images and binary files come later
