title: Better error messages when an agent tries to update a public-view item or hits a permission wall
description:
## Problem
2026-04-07 onboarding test: agent tried to update a `public` (view-only) item and got a generic 403 or "not found" error with no explanation that the issue was VISIBILITY (not authentication).

## Goal
Every permission-related error response should explain:
1. What the agent tried to do
2. Why it was rejected (specifically: which permission rule)
3. What the agent could do instead

## Current state (audit needed)
Need to grep api.py for all 403 / "not found" responses related to write operations and confirm they include a specific reason field.

## Proposed error format

```json
{
  "error": "permission_denied",
  "reason": "item_visibility_does_not_allow_edit",
  "message": "This item has visibility=public (read-only). Only the owner can edit. To allow others to edit, the owner must change visibility to public_edit.",
  "item": {
    "slug": "...",
    "owner": "...",
    "visibility": "public"
  },
  "alternatives": [
    "Ask the owner to change visibility",
    "Fork the item to your own account",
    "Use POST /items/new to create a new item"
  ]
}
```

## Specific cases to handle clearly
- public item, non-owner tries to PUT/PATCH/append/edit → "this is read-only public, owner only"
- private item, anyone non-owner tries to access → "this item is private, only the owner can see it"
- shared item, user not in share list tries to access → "this is shared with specific users, you do not have access"
- public_edit item, unauthenticated user tries to edit → "you must sign in to edit public_edit items"
- write_scope missing → "your API key has read-only scope, regenerate with write scope"

## Surfaces to update
- api.py: every 403/404 in items/* and edit endpoints
- views.py: same, plus the public_edit_item view
- templates/item.html: maybe show a "this is read-only" badge for non-owners viewing public items

## Related
- 2026-04-07 onboarding test
- listhub-9nm (visibility label consistency)
- listhub-rmb (auth migration — error contracts should be defined as part of the migration)

title: Username auto-suggestion endpoint + smarter default for agent registrations
description:
## Problem
2026-04-07 onboarding test: agent registered with a generic username like "codex" or its agent name, instead of a human-friendly name representing the human owner. The result is profile pages with weird names attached to a real person's content.

## Goal
Make it easy for an agent to pick a sensible username for the human user it represents.

## Two complementary fixes

### Fix 1: Username suggestion endpoint
GET /api/v1/auth/suggest-username?email=jacob@example.com&display_name=Jacob+Cole

Returns:
{
  "suggestions": ["jacob", "jacobcole", "jcole", "jacob-cole"],
  "available": ["jacob", "jcole"],
  "first_available": "jacob"
}

The agent calls this BEFORE registering, picks an available suggestion, and uses it for the actual registration.

### Fix 2: Documentation prompt
In llms.txt and the agent onboarding instructions, explicitly say:

> When registering on behalf of a human user, use the human user's name for the username, NOT your own agent name. For example: if a user named Sarah Chen asks you to set up ListHub, register as `sarah` or `sarahchen`, not `claude` or `assistant`.

This is a documentation fix that costs nothing but probably eliminates the issue for most agents.

## Open questions
- Should the registration endpoint REJECT obviously-agent usernames like "claude", "gpt", "codex", "assistant"? Probably no — heavy-handed.
- Should it warn? Maybe — return a `warning` field in the response.
- Should the suggestion endpoint also propose modifications if the first choice is taken? Yes (the suggestions list above).

## Related
- 2026-04-07 onboarding test
- listhub-480 (onboarding skill / clearer instructions)
- listhub-2jg (closed) — agent self-service onboarding

title: Publishable curated directory UI (like flowtools.directory)
description:
Build a UI that lets users create and publish curated directories — like flowtools.directory but hosted on ListHub. A directory is a special list type where each entry has structured metadata (name, URL, description, category, tags, logo).

Features:
- Category filtering and search
- Card-based layout with logos/thumbnails
- Submission form for community contributions
- Public URL for sharing the directory

Implementation options:
- Special item_type='directory' with structured entries in the content
- Collection of linked items with a directory template applied (depends on listhub-bd1 template system)
- Structured frontmatter/JSON schema for directory entry metadata

Related tickets:
- listhub-bd1: View template system (this is a specific application of that system, focused on the directory/catalog use case)
- listhub-00k: Flowtools.directory import would be the first content powering this directory UI

The directory view is one of the most compelling use cases for ListHub — turning a simple list into a browsable, searchable, visually rich catalog.

title: Agent-native Google Drive replacement (markdown-first uploads + format converters)
description:
## Vision
ListHub as a markdown-first, agent-native alternative to Google Drive. Keeps what Drive does well (folder-based organization, sharing permissions, browser file management) but centers everything around markdown — which is 10x more agent-friendly than Google Docs blocks.

## Positioning vs Google Drive
| | Google Drive | ListHub |
|---|---|---|
| Primary content format | Proprietary blocks (Docs, Sheets) | **Markdown files** |
| Agent-readable | Via API with heavy auth | Plain text with YAML frontmatter |
| Git-friendly | No | **Yes**, content-addressed git repos per user |
| Self-service agent signup | OAuth dance | `POST /api/v1/auth/register` |
| Per-item visibility | Yes | Yes + agent-settable at write time |
| Per-folder permissions | Yes | Yes (when listhub-74j ships) |
| Private content within a public doc | No | **`<!-- private -->` blocks** |
| Public URL for any item | Share dance | `/@user/slug` instantly |
| Native image/PDF support | Yes | **Coming** (listhub-c6x, listhub-2zv) |
| Document format conversion | Native (Docs is the editor) | **Import via converters** |

## Child tickets (this epic)
- **listhub-ma7** — Markdown file upload (drag-drop .md files) — the trivial starting point
- **listhub-dwa** — Document converter: docx / doc / rtf → markdown on import
- **listhub-7c5** — Google Drive connector: one-click Drive folder import
- **listhub-67v** — Bulk folder/vault import UI (drag a folder into the browser)
- **listhub-c6x** — Inline image support (Obsidian-style)
- **listhub-2zv** — File bucket / upload architecture decision
- **listhub-74j** — Folder-level visibility with cascade (the permissions foundation)

## Already shipped (supporting this vision)
- Per-item visibility with agent-settable values
- Git-backed markdown with YAML frontmatter
- Programmatic registration (`POST /auth/register`)
- llms.txt for agent discovery
- Home page per user (special `home` slug)
- Private content blocks (`<!-- private -->`)
- Folder drill-down navigation
- Import tooling (`scripts/import_markdown.py`)

## Success criteria
A user can:
1. Register their agent account in one curl call
2. Drop their entire Obsidian vault or Drive folder into ListHub
3. See it rendered with proper folder hierarchy
4. Mix public and private items freely
5. Set folder-level permissions that cascade to children
6. Give an agent a Bearer token and have it navigate, read, create, update items
7. Clone their whole account as a git repo for offline work

When all seven are smooth, ListHub IS an agent-native Drive.

## Anti-goals
- **Not a Google Docs competitor** — we are not building a rich text editor with real-time collaboration. Milkdown handles markdown editing; thats enough.
- **Not a shared drive for enterprise** — team accounts are future work, not the core pitch.
- **Not native mobile apps** — PWA is fine.

## Related
- listhub-7aw — Pioneer the agent-first web (sibling epic covering the standard/discovery side)
- listhub-xtc — GitHub-like repo UX (the clone/download/history side)

title: Unified metadata-in-YAML pattern: frontmatter for markdown, .meta.yaml sidecar for binaries, .folder.yaml for folders
description:
## Vision
One unifying principle: **"Every piece of metadata in ListHub lives in YAML files inside the git repo."** The content type determines the filename convention, but the format, location, and semantics are consistent.

## Three filename conventions, one mental model

| Content type | Metadata location | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Markdown item** | YAML **frontmatter inside** the `.md` file | Self-contained LLM memory unit; travels with the content |
| **Folder** | `.folder.yaml` **in** the folder | Cant embed frontmatter in a directory; this is the nearest equivalent |
| **Binary file** (PDF, image, audio, video) | `foo.meta.yaml` **next to** the binary | Cant embed YAML in arbitrary binary formats |

All three are plain YAML. All three are git-tracked. All three are agent-writable. All three use the same field vocabulary (`visibility`, `title`, `tags`, `created_at`, etc.).

## Why this matters

### For the markdown-first vision (listhub-r62)
Binary file support would be a *compromise* on "markdown-native" if we stored metadata in a DB-only way. But if binary files get a **sidecar YAML file** in the same repo, then:
- The markdown-first aesthetic is preserved (you can still clone a users repo and see everything in text files)
- Binaries become first-class without sacrificing the self-contained ethos
- Agents can read/write metadata for BOTH markdown and binary with the same mental model

### For the self-contained LLM memory use case
Markdown files stay fully self-contained — you can download one `.md`, paste it into a new tool, and the privacy/provenance travels with it. This is THE NEW PRIMARY use of markdown in the LLM era (Karpathy-style personal knowledge wikis).

### For git-native workflow
Everything lives in the git repo as plain text. Git diffs show metadata changes clearly. `git log -p` shows when visibility changed. `git blame` shows who changed it.

## Source of truth + cache (redundancy is fine)

- **Source of truth**: the YAML file (frontmatter or sidecar). When the DB and the file disagree, the file wins.
- **Cache**: the SQLite DB row is a derived cache for query performance. On `git push`, the post-receive hook parses the file and rebuilds the cache.

This is the same pattern Jekyll, Hugo, Obsidian, and most static-site generators use. Well-understood, works well.

## Binary storage (separate from metadata)

Binary blobs do NOT live in the git repo — they would bloat clones. They live in a blob store keyed by sha256:

```
LISTHUB_BLOB_ROOT/
├── ab/
│   └── abc123def456...  (actual PDF bytes)
```

The git repo contains only the `.meta.yaml` sidecar, which references the blob by hash. Download protocol: `listhub pull-blobs` or lazy fetch on access. git-lfs could be added later as a transparency layer.

## Example sidecar

```yaml
# attachments/body-masters-original.pdf.meta.yaml
title: Body Masters Spreadsheet (original Google Sheets export)
slug: body-masters-original-pdf
visibility: public
item_type: attachment
tags: [bodywork, archive, original-export]
created_at: 2026-04-07T12:00:00Z
updated_at: 2026-04-07T12:00:00Z
file:
  name: body-masters-original.pdf
  size: 1240832
  sha256: abc123def456...
  mime_type: application/pdf
  blob_path: ab/abc123def456...
description: |
  Full original Google Sheets export of the Body Masters list before
  it was imported as markdown. Preserved for archival reference.
```

Note how the fields are a superset of what a markdown frontmatter would contain — same `visibility`, `tags`, `title` — plus file-specific fields nested under `file:`.

## Design decisions

- [ ] **Sidecar naming**: `foo.pdf.meta.yaml` (double extension) vs `foo.pdf.yaml` (replace) vs `foo.meta.yaml` (strip extension). I recommend **double extension** (`foo.pdf.meta.yaml`) so the original filename is preserved and sorting groups related files.
- [ ] **Blob storage backend**: local disk (start), S3/R2, IPFS (future). Covered by listhub-2zv.
- [ ] **Cascade inheritance**: non-markdown files inherit visibility from their containing folder (same rule as markdown items). Explicit sidecar values override.
- [ ] **What counts as "binary"**: anything not ending in `.md`, `.yaml`, or `.folder.yaml`. Could make exceptions for `.txt`/`.rst`/`.org` later.
- [ ] **Sidecar visibility**: the sidecar YAML files themselves are server-side metadata and should NOT be listed as browsable items (like `.folder.yaml`, like `.gitignore`).

## Relationship to existing tickets

- **listhub-r62** (Agent-native Google Drive epic) — parent; this is the metadata foundation
- **listhub-2zv** (File bucket architecture) — the WHERE question for blob storage; independent of this ticket
- **listhub-c6x** (Inline image support) — will use this sidecar pattern for image metadata
- **listhub-dwa** (docx converter) — converted docx becomes `.md`, so uses existing frontmatter path
- **listhub-74j** (Folder visibility with cascade) — same sidecar philosophy, `.folder.yaml` is the folder-level instance of this pattern
- **listhub-f18** (Home page private/public split) — complements this at the item level

## Answer to the original framing
> "Are we losing our way by making something too far from markdown-native?"

**No** — this pattern REINFORCES the markdown-first vision. Markdown stays first-class with inline frontmatter. Binaries get a sidecar that follows the same YAML philosophy. Both are git-tracked, both are agent-readable, both are self-documenting.

The alternative (DB-only metadata for binaries) would be the one that breaks the aesthetic. We should not do that.

## No real blockers
This is pure design work plus the sidecar reader/writer code. No schema changes to the DB required until the binary upload feature actually ships (at which point we add a `file_attachment` table that references both the blob and the sidecar path).

title: API-key login: paste-your-key as a first-class web auth method
description:
## Goal
Add a third auth surface alongside Noos OAuth and (deprecated) local password login: paste your API key into a textarea to sign in. This collapses several use cases into one mechanism and likely lets us delete /login/local entirely.

## Why this is the right shape

1. It IS the agent identity model already. Agents authenticate via Bearer tokens. All we are adding is an alternate input surface (textarea instead of HTTP header). The credential is identical.

2. It bridges agent and human modes of the same account. An agent registers via POST /api/v1/auth/register and creates items. The human owner pastes the same key into a browser → instant session as that account → can review/edit/delete what the agent did.

3. It collapses three problems into one mechanism:
   - Recovery from lost key → use any other key (or use POST /auth/token with credentials)
   - Browser access for agent accounts → paste the key
   - Noos-down emergency → paste your key

## UX

A small page at /login/key (or as a tab on /login):

- Single textarea input
- "Sign in" button
- Brief help text: "Paste a key from POST /api/v1/auth/register or get one in Settings"

Server side: POST /login/key with body {key: "mem_..."} → validate against api_key.key_hash → look up user → flask_login.login_user() → redirect to dashboard.

## Why this lets us deprecate /login/local

| Use case for password login | API-key replacement |
|---|---|
| Agent web access | Paste agent key |
| Noos-down emergency | Paste any existing key |
| Dev / preview instance | Paste a dev key |
| Pre-Noos legacy users | Migrate via POST /auth/token once, then key-only forever |
| Lost key recovery | POST /auth/token with username+password → new key |

After API-key login ships, /login/local becomes a one-time-use migration path for legacy accounts, then deletable.

## Three-tier auth model (revised)

1. **Noos OAuth** — primary path for human users at /login. One click.
2. **API key paste** — fallback: agents accessing via browser, emergencies, dev instances, recovery. /login/key.
3. **Local username/password** — DEPRECATED. /login/local exists for migration window only.

## Implementation
- New route: GET/POST /login/key in auth.py
- New template: templates/login_key.html (single textarea + submit)
- Reuse existing api_key validation logic from auth.py require_api_auth
- On success: flask_login.login_user(user, remember=True)
- Add link from /login choice page (when added) and from llms.txt + api/docs

## Security considerations
- API key is sensitive — show paste textarea, NOT a normal text input (so it does not autofill)
- Rate limit /login/key to prevent brute force (it is a 40-char random token, but still)
- Log successful API-key logins to the user activity feed so the owner can see when their key was used
- Consider: warn the user "this key has been used to sign in via browser at <timestamp>, was that you?"

## Related
- listhub-rmb (Noos auth migration epic) — this is part of that plan
- listhub-bwl (API key recovery) — same family of fixes
- listhub-2jg (closed) — agent self-service onboarding
- 2026-04-07 onboarding test that surfaced the gap

title: Migrate authentication to Noos centralized auth
description:
## Goal
Decide and implement the auth model for ListHub. Triggered by 2026-04-07 onboarding test where an agent registered via POST /api/v1/auth/register but then could not figure out how to web-log-in (because /login redirects to Noos, not local).

## The decision (RECOMMENDED: Path B — Noos primary, local as quiet fallback)

### Path B: Noos primary, local as silent fallback
- /login continues hard-redirecting to Noos OAuth (CURRENT BEHAVIOR — keep it)
- /login/local exists but is NOT linked from any UI
- /login/local is the emergency exit + dev preview path
- Agents NEVER web-log-in. They use API keys (Bearer tokens) exclusively.
- The fix for the failing agent is NOT to change auth UX — its to make the
  API-key flow work without web login.

### Why this over Path A (Noos-only, kill local login)
- Need a fallback when Noos is down
- Need preview/dev instances to work without Noos integration
- Pre-Noos legacy password accounts still need access (can be migrated over time)

### Why this over Path C (show both on /login)
- Adds visual clutter
- Conflicts with the "Noos is the way" goal
- Encourages users to pick the wrong path

## The actual gap the agent hit (this is the real fix)

After registering via POST /api/v1/auth/register, the agent gets an API key shown ONCE. If the agent loses that key, there is no recovery path. The agent CANNOT:
- Get the API key again from username+password
- Web-log-in to retrieve the key from settings
- Recover access at all

### Required: API key recovery from username + password

Add a new endpoint:

POST /api/v1/auth/key (or /tokens or /keys/from-credentials)
Body: {"username": "...", "password": "...", "name": "recovery"}
Response: {"key": "mem_...", "key_id": "...", "name": "recovery"}

This is essentially the same as POST /auth/token but explicitly framed as "give me a NEW key using my credentials" — the existing /auth/token endpoint already does this, but it is poorly discoverable and the docs do not call it out as the key-recovery path.

ACTION: rename, document, and prominently feature this endpoint as "API key recovery" so agents that lose their key can recover.

## Implementation impact (when migrating to Noos-only is decided)
- auth.py: simplify login() to single Noos redirect (already done)
- /login/local stays
- For password accounts: migration script that links them to Noos identities by email when they next sign in
- Eventually: drop the password column or set all passwords to "!noos-oauth"
- git_backend.py: _check_basic_auth still validates against ListHub API keys, NOT against Noos directly. Git auth is API-key-only.

## Related
- 2026-04-07 onboarding test session (Jacob)
- listhub-r62 (agent-native Drive replacement) — depends on smooth agent onboarding
- listhub-7aw (agent-first web epic)

title: Folder zoom: full-screen focused folder view (modal or dedicated page)
description:
## Goal
A way to "zoom in" on a single folder and focus only on its contents, with a clean view.

## Current state
- /@user/folder-path/ already shows a folder drill-down page (folder.html) with breadcrumbs and child listing
- But the parent profile/dashboard layout is still around it (header, footer)
- There is no full-screen / modal "focus" mode where just the folder fills the viewport

## Proposed designs

### Option A: Dedicated maximize button on the folder page
- A maximize icon on /@user/folder-path/ that opens the folder in a stripped-down layout
- ESC to exit back to normal view
- URL: ?focus=1 query param

### Option B: True modal overlay
- Click a folder anywhere to open an overlay that fills the viewport
- ESC closes the overlay
- Direct link via /@user/folder-path/?modal=1 for sharing

### Option C: New focus-mode layout
- Hide the left sidebar entirely when in focus mode
- Push content to full width
- Add a "back to dashboard" button instead of the sidebar

## Why
- When you have a folder with 50+ items, the surrounding chrome competes for attention
- Want a presentation-quality view for sharing a single folder publicly
- Matches Finder "Open in new window", Notion "Open as page", GitHub "View source" full-screen modes

## Related
- listhub-kdg - Folder drill-down (already wired)
- listhub-74j - Folder visibility

title: Save/favorite lists you like
description:
## Goal
Let users save/bookmark/favorite public lists and items from other users. This is a core social feature — like starring a GitHub repo or saving a tweet.

## User Stories
- As a user browsing Explore, I want to save a list I like so I can find it later
- As a user viewing someone's profile, I want to favorite their best items
- As a user, I want to see all my saved items in one place on my dashboard
- As an agent, I want to programmatically save items for my user

## Design

### UI Elements

**Save button on public items:**
- Small bookmark icon on item cards (Explore, Directory, profile pages)
- Toggle: click to save, click again to unsave
- Show save count on items (like GitHub stars)
- Filled bookmark = saved, outline = not saved

**Saved items view:**
- New tab on dashboard: "Saved" (alongside existing filters)
- Or: dedicated /saved route
- Shows all saved items with the original author visible
- Sortable by: date saved, date updated, most popular

**Save count on items:**
- Show "12 saves" or a star count on public item pages
- Optional: show on item cards in explore view

### Data Model

```sql
CREATE TABLE saved_item (
    user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES user(id),
    item_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES item(id),
    saved_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (user_id, item_id)
);
```

### API Endpoints

```
POST   /api/v1/items/:id/save     — save an item (requires auth)
DELETE /api/v1/items/:id/save     — unsave an item (requires auth)
GET    /api/v1/saved              — list all saved items (requires auth)
GET    /api/v1/items/:id          — include save_count + is_saved in response
```

### Implementation Notes
- Only public items can be saved (shared items only if you have access)
- Saving doesn't copy the item — it's a reference (like a bookmark)
- If the original author makes the item private, it disappears from your saved list
- Save count could be used for ranking in Explore (most-saved items surface higher)
- Agent API: agents should be able to save items on behalf of their user

## Open Questions
- Should saves be public? (e.g., "12 people saved this" vs anonymous count)
- Should there be collections/folders for saved items? (like Twitter bookmarks folders)
- Notifications: should authors be notified when their item is saved?

title: Implement WebMCP for ListHub (browser agent tools)
Navigation consistency audit: find inconsistencies across dashboard/profile/explore/folder/item views
description:
## Audit findings (2026-04-07)

Systematic scan of navigation behaviors across all main views.

### Consistencies (good)

- All item pages use the same URL pattern `/@username/slug`.
- Folder pages use `/@username/path/` with trailing slash (`user_folder`
  route at views.py:267).
- Profile and dashboard folder views both use `tree.html` — consistent
  tree rendering.
- `item.html` has breadcrumbs via `_build_user_breadcrumbs()`.
- `edit` button logic is centralized enough.

### Inconsistencies found

- [ ] **TWO tree implementations**: `tree.html` (used by dash+profile) and
  `_sidebar_tree.html` (used by sidebar). Different class prefixes
  (`tree-*` vs `sb-*`), different click behaviors (row-click vs chevron
  button), different folder link patterns. See listhub-d2z.
- [x] **tree.html had no folder drill-down link** — fixed in cc768f3 (kdg).
- [ ] **dash.html list view visibility badge icons vs profile.html**:
  dash.html uses 🌐/🔗/🔒 for visibility in list view, profile.html uses
  ✏️/🌐/🔗/🔒 (includes public_edit). Unify the symbol set and logic.
- [ ] **Shared filter label says "Shared (WIP)"** in dash.html — either
  finish the feature or remove the "(WIP)" suffix for polish.
- [ ] **community.html vs directory.html**: two templates. Is one legacy?
  Are both reachable? Need to check routes and decide if they should
  merge.
- [ ] **Published filter tab** added in cc768f3 (u5i) but sits awkwardly
  between "All" and "Public". Consider reordering: All / Private /
  Published / Public / Public Edit / Shared — with Published as a
  shortcut to non-private.
- [ ] **Profile header inconsistency**: /@username now has a Home button
  (ftr, cc768f3). /dash does not have any equivalent entry to a
  user-controlled landing. Decide whether dash should also link to /home.
- [ ] **Mobile behavior of sidebar tree**: not yet audited. Need to check
  whether `_sidebar.html` collapses to a drawer on narrow viewports.
  File a child ticket if missing.
- [ ] **Search scope is inconsistent**: dash search is scoped to owner,
  browse/explore searches public items. Users may not notice which one
  they are in. Add subtitle or placeholder text clarifying scope.
- [ ] **No consistent empty state component**: dash.html, profile.html,
  browse.html, explore.html each render an empty-state div differently.
  Consolidate into an `_empty.html` partial.
- [ ] **Login/register pages render sidebar**: if `_sidebar.html` is
  included in `base.html`, it may render on auth pages where it has no
  context. Verify and skip sidebar on auth routes.
- [ ] **Edit button logic still has three code paths** — see listhub-60j.

## Child tickets to file (as you decide to tackle these)

- Consolidate tree.html and _sidebar_tree.html into one component
- Audit empty states across all list views
- Audit search scope messaging
- Verify sidebar on auth pages
- Fix "Shared (WIP)" label
- Unify visibility badge symbols across views

## Status
Audit complete — findings documented. Some fixes landed (kdg, partial d2z,
u5i, ftr). The rest should be filed as child tickets when you decide to
prioritize them. This ticket can stay open as the parent audit or be closed
once child tickets are created.

title: Import Moltbook harvested ideas and knowledge into ListHub
description:
## Goal
Import the processed/harvested ideas and knowledge from the Moltbook archive into ListHub as public lists and knowledge documents.

## Source Data
Moltbook was an AI agent social network (Meta) with ~50K posts from Jan 27 – Mar 13, 2026. Jacob's team scraped the full archive before shutdown. Processed knowledge lives in two locations:

### On Lightsail (noos-prod: /home/ubuntu/noos/)
- `knowledge/00-overview.md` — Summary of the Moltbook analysis
- `knowledge/01-qa-bank.md` — Questions and answers
- `knowledge/02-idea-bank.md` — Curated ideas (Nightly Build pattern, Email-to-Podcast, etc.)
- `knowledge/03-open-problems.md` — Unsolved problems from agent community
- `knowledge/04-noos-relevant.md` — Knowledge graph architecture patterns
- `knowledge/05-agent-culture.md` — Agent culture and social dynamics
- `knowledge/06-phase2-report.md` through `11-goldmine-practical-workflows.md` — Phase 2 deep analysis
- `knowledge/11-goldmine-practical-workflows.md` — **Most valuable**: proven workflows, working architectures, operational lessons

### On local machine
- `~/code/noos/moltbook-archive/` — Raw archive data, extraction scripts
- `~/code/noos/knowledge/` — Same processed docs (mirrored)

## Highlights to Import
- **Idea Bank** (02): Agent ideas scored by upvotes — Nightly Build (451 upvotes), Email-to-Podcast, etc.
- **Goldmine Practical Workflows** (11): Three-layer memory pattern, agent architectures, operational patterns
- **Open Problems** (03): Unsolved challenges in agent systems
- **Feature Requests by Project** (09): What agents wanted from various tools
- **External Suggestions** (10): Agent community suggestions for real-world products

## ListHub Structure
- Each knowledge doc → one ListHub item (note or document type)
- Tag: moltbook, agent-culture, knowledge-graph, etc.
- Visibility: public (this is unique, valuable knowledge)
- Could become a "Moltbook Archive" directory entry

## Context
See memory file: moltbook-archive.md for full archive details (3.3 GB raw, ~2M records)

title: User account rename: change username, display_name, email after signup (UI + API)
description:
## Goal
Let users change their account fields after signup, both via the web UI and programmatically via the API. The three editable fields:

- **`display_name`** — easy. Just an UPDATE on the user table. No side effects.
- **`email`** — easy. UPDATE + uniqueness check.
- **`username`** — hard. URL changes, repo path changes, all shared links break.

## Why this matters
2026-04-07 onboarding test surfaced multiple cases where the agent registered with a sub-optimal username (e.g., "codex" instead of the human user's name). Today there is NO way to fix this without deleting and re-creating the account, which loses all items, history, and shared URLs.

Also a more general need: people change their preferred display names, change email addresses, and occasionally want to rename their handle. Every account system supports this; we should too.

## Easy: display_name + email

### API
```
PUT /api/v1/auth/me
{"display_name": "Jacob Cole", "email": "jacob@example.com"}
```

Returns the updated user object. Validates email uniqueness if provided.

### UI
A settings page section "Profile" with two text fields. Saves via the API endpoint above.

### Implementation
- One DB UPDATE
- Update Noos linkage if email changes (it's the bridge for auto-link)
- No cascading changes needed

## Hard: username rename

This is the interesting part because the username is part of every URL and the git repo path.

### What changes when username changes
- `/@oldname/...` URLs no longer work (or should redirect)
- `/git/oldname.git` bare repo path changes (or needs symlink)
- All previously-shared links to items break
- All `file_path` values are unaffected (they're relative)
- All API keys remain valid (they're keyed by user_id, not username)
- Sidebar / search / navigation still works (also keyed by user_id)

### Proposed redirect strategy
Add a `username_history` table:
```sql
CREATE TABLE username_history (
    user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES user(id),
    old_username TEXT NOT NULL,
    new_username TEXT NOT NULL,
    changed_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (user_id, old_username)
);
```

On every request to `/@<name>/...`, the user_profile / public_item / user_folder views first try the current username. If not found, look up `old_username` in `username_history` → 301 redirect to the new URL.

This means **old links always work**. They just redirect.

### Git repo path strategy
The bare repo currently lives at `<REPO_ROOT>/<username>.git`. On rename:
- Option A: rename the repo directory (simple but breaks any clones already pointing at the old path)
- Option B: leave the repo at its original location, look it up by user_id instead of username (requires rewriting `_repo_path()` to take a user_id)
- Option C: rename the directory AND create a symlink at the old name (best of both)

I recommend **Option B** — store `repo_dir` in the user table, set it on initial create, and never change it on rename. The directory stays at `<REPO_ROOT>/<original_username>.git` even if the user renames. Old git clones keep working without symlinks. New clones use the current `/git/<username>.git` URL which the server resolves to the user's `repo_dir`.

### Username collision handling
- Reject if the new username is currently in use by another active user (case-insensitive)
- Reject if the new username is in another user's `username_history` from the last 90 days (avoid impersonation via squatting)
- The reservation period is configurable

### API
```
PUT /api/v1/auth/me/username
{"username": "newname"}
```

Returns:
- `200` with the updated user
- `409 Conflict` if the username is taken
- `403` if the new name is reserved or invalid

### UI
A settings page section "Username" with:
- Current username display
- Text field with availability check (debounced as you type)
- A clear warning: "Changing your username will redirect old URLs but break direct API references that use the username instead of the user ID"
- Confirm button

## Validation rules (same as registration)
- Alphanumeric only (matches existing register validation)
- 2+ characters
- Case-insensitive uniqueness

## Audit trail
Every rename should be logged:
- Who changed it
- When
- Old name → new name
- Reason (optional, free text)

This is for both compliance (impersonation detection) and user-visible history ("you renamed from `codex` to `jacob` on 2026-04-09").

## Programmatic / agent considerations
- Agents that registered with bad usernames can be fixed later via this endpoint
- The agent's API key continues to work — it's keyed by user_id not username
- Onboarding flow improvement: after registration, the response could include a hint like `"username_changeable_at": "/api/v1/auth/me/username"` so agents know it's not permanent

## Related
- listhub-obd — Username auto-suggestion endpoint (preventive: pick better names at signup)
- listhub-bwl — API key recovery (related: account management endpoints)
- listhub-rmb — Noos auth migration (this should ship as part of the broader auth/account management story)
- listhub-r7u — API-key login (uses user_id under the hood, won't break on rename)
- 2026-04-07 onboarding test
- listhub-3cm — Inline permissions and quick actions (the settings page work overlaps with this)

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `PUT /api/v1/auth/me` updates display_name and email
- [ ] `PUT /api/v1/auth/me/username` renames username, validates uniqueness + reservation period
- [ ] `username_history` table exists, populated on rename
- [ ] Old `/@<oldname>/...` URLs redirect 301 to new `/@<newname>/...`
- [ ] Git clones at the original repo path keep working (Option B)
- [ ] Settings page has a Profile section with all three fields
- [ ] Agents can rename via API with their existing key
- [ ] Audit trail logged for every rename

title: Add Published or Non-private dashboard view (shared+public+public_edit in one)
description:
## Goal
Right now the dashboard /dash?visibility=public filter shows only public
items, and similarly for shared/public_edit/private. There is no way to
see "everything I have published" (any non-private) in one view.

## Proposal
Add a /dash?visibility=published (or a separate /dash/published) that
unions: public + public_edit + shared.

Or: add an additional filter tab "Published" alongside All/Private/
Public/Shared/Public-edit.

## Why

  which may or may not be unlisted, or would have you yet, or no?"

  /dash?visibility=public_edit and /dash?visibility=shared to see

title: Better 404 page when URLs get moved or deleted
description:
## Problem
When a URL moves, gets renamed, or the item is deleted, visitors get a raw Flask 404 page with no context and no way to recover. Example flagged by Jacob:

`https://listhub.globalbr.ai/@jacobreal/startx-anythinggoes-2025-05-02-startx-anythingg-startx-board-of-advisors-help-aasdfasdf`

(long slug from the StartX wiki import — not found, hard fail)

This is a bad first impression for anyone clicking a shared link that has gone stale.

## Proposed design

A custom 404 template (`templates/404.html`) that:

### When the URL is `/@username/<slug>`
- Shows "This page doesn't exist on @username's ListHub" with the username
- Runs a server-side slug-similarity search against the owner's items and suggests the top 3-5 closest matches
- Shows the owner's last 5 recently updated public items as a fallback
- Offers a link to the owner's profile `/@username` and to the owner's home item if present
- Offers a search box scoped to that user

### When the URL is `/@username/<path:subpath>/` (folder)
- Same thing but for folder paths — suggest closest folder match, link to parent folder, link to profile

### When the URL is `/@username` (user doesn't exist)
- Suggest similar usernames from the user table
- Link to People page, Explore, Directory

### Generic 404
- Global default: friendly message, explore + search CTAs, link to home

## Implementation notes
- Flask: `@app.errorhandler(404)` in `app.py` that catches and renders `404.html`
- Slug similarity: SQLite LIKE + Python `difflib.get_close_matches()` against the user's items filtered by visibility
- For huge item lists, cap the candidate search at ~500 items
- Remember to check: the rendered suggestions should respect visibility (don't leak private slugs in the suggestion list — only suggest items the visitor can actually see)
- Style to match the ListHub aesthetic (GitHub-style 404 with octocat is a good reference — playful but informative)

## Bonus ideas
- **Redirect history**: when an item's slug changes, log the old slug → new slug mapping and auto-redirect old URLs. Related to the upcoming slug-rename feature.
- **Wayback fallback**: for items that were deleted, show "This item was deleted. [See archived version on Wayback](...)" if a snapshot exists.
- **Author contact**: if the item is 404 but the author exists, offer "Ask @username for the updated link."

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `templates/404.html` exists and is rendered on all 404s
- [ ] For `/@username/slug` 404s, similar slugs are suggested
- [ ] For `/@username` 404s, similar usernames are suggested
- [ ] Private items never appear in public suggestions
- [ ] The page has working search + explore links
- [ ] Style matches the rest of ListHub

## Related
- StartX wiki import — generated very long slugs that are easy to mistype
- Slug rename feature (future) — when shipped, should auto-redirect old slugs

title: Auto-generate slug from list title
description: When creating a new item, auto-generate the slug from the title (slugify). Currently slug must be manually provided.

title: Folder view (GitHub-style auto-listing + optional README + shared breadcrumbs)
description:
## Goal Add a GitHub-style folder view so visiting `/@user/folder/` (trailing slash) shows an auto-generated listing of the folder's children, optionally with a README rendered below it. Also add breadcrumbs consistently across every content page (folder, item, community directory). ## Key decision: GitHub's actual pattern (not our overcomplicated earlier proposals) After thinking about this more carefully with Jacob pushing back on the earlier "hybrid" and "first-class folder object" proposals, the right answer is the simplest one: **copy what GitHub does**. GitHub folder view = 1. Breadcrumbs at top 2. Auto-listing of children (folders first, then files) 3. If a child named README exists, render its content below the listing 4. Nothing else. No folder entity. No special metadata file. ## Why Option A (auto-listing only) is right Earlier brainstorm dismissed "pure auto-listing" as "cold, no context, can't explain what the folder is for." All three concerns are wrong: - "Cold" — GitHub folders are cold and nobody minds. The listing IS the context. - "No context" — item names ARE the context. A folder containing `transformers.md`, `attention.md`, `mamba.md` tells you exactly what it's about. - "Can't explain what the folder is for" — when you need to, add a README. Not required. So the correct model is: auto-listing is the primary content, README is an optional enhancement. Zero new concepts to teach users. ## Data model: no change - Folders are still derived from `file_path` on existing items - A "folder" = the set of items whose `file_path` starts with a given prefix - No new table, no new item_type, no migration - The README is a totally normal item with slug=README that happens to get auto-rendered on the parent folder page - Visibility is derived: non-owner sees only public children, owner sees everything with badges ## URLs - `/@jacobreal` — profile page (existing, unchanged) - `/@jacobreal/research/` — folder view (trailing slash distinguishes from item) - `/@jacobreal/research/ai/` — nested folder view - `/@jacobreal/research/ai/transformers` — item inside a folder - Flask's strict_slashes handles the trailing-slash disambiguation naturally ## Page rendering ### Folder view (`templates/folder.html`) 1. Breadcrumbs (shared partial) 2. Folder title (last path segment) 3. Listing table: subfolders first (with child count + aggregate visibility), then items (with title, visibility badge, last-updated) 4. Optional README: if an item with `slug=README` exists at this path, render its content below the listing 5. Owner action bar: "+ New in this folder" button (prefilled file_path) 6. Non-owner sees only public/public_edit children; folder 404 if no visible children ### Item pages get breadcrumbs too Every item page (`templates/item.html`) renders the shared breadcrumb partial at the top: `@jacobreal / research / ai / transformers` ### Community directory refactor `templates/directory.html` already has breadcrumbs. Refactor to use the shared partial so style and behavior are consistent everywhere. ## Breadcrumb partial (`templates/_breadcrumbs.html`) ```jinja {# crumbs = [{name, url}, ...] — last item has url=None for current #}
{% for crumb in crumbs %} {% if crumb.url %}{{ crumb.name }} {% else %}{{ crumb.name }}{% endif %} {% if not loop.last %}/{% endif %} {% endfor %}
``` ## V1 scope 1. **Route**: `/@//` with trailing slash 2. **Resolver in views.py**: filter items by file_path prefix, group into direct children 3. **Folder template**: breadcrumbs + listing + optional README 4. **Item template**: add breadcrumbs at top 5. **Community directory**: refactor to use shared breadcrumbs 6. **CSS**: breadcrumb styling ## V1.5 / V2 (defer) - Sidebar folder name as link to folder view (requires restructuring
/ interaction) - Collision enforcement on API create (reject slug that collides with subfolder name) - Root folder view merging with profile page (currently `/@user/` → profile; could become folder view + profile header) - Batch-update file_path (for folder renames) - Custom child ordering / pinning - Per-folder cover images or icons ## Sequencing Build on branch `feature/folder-view` off main. Ship for preview without merging. Jacob reviews live. If approved, merge to main. ## Related tickets - `listhub-lav` — Three-section sidebar (V1.1 shipped; folder view adds the missing navigation destination) - `listhub-xtc` — GitHub-like repo UX (this is part of that) - `listhub-60j` — Consolidate edit experiences (will eventually touch the folder edit flow) - `listhub-7aw.2` — llms-txt.io directory integration (unrelated but both about indexing content)

title: Add file_path move API: PATCH /api/v1/items/:id/move
description:
## Goal
Add an API endpoint to update an items file_path so items can be moved between folders without delete-and-recreate.

## Current state
- PUT /api/v1/items/:id supports updating title, slug, visibility, item_type, tags
- PUT /api/v1/items/by-slug/:slug supports updating content, title, visibility, item_type, tags
- NEITHER updates file_path
- Result: agents and scripts cannot move items between folders. Today the only way is direct DB UPDATE, which bypasses git_sync and leaves orphaned files in the bare repo.

## Proposed
Extend PUT /api/v1/items/:id to accept file_path as another updatable field. After the DB update commits, also call git_sync to:
1. Read content of old file from bare repo
2. Write to new file path with same content
3. Delete old file
4. Single commit with message "move: old to new"

## Use cases
- listhub-aeu (audit imported docs) needs this for clean reorganization
- Future folder reorganization features
- Drag-drop file moves in a future UI

## Related
- listhub-aeu - uses this to reorganize the systematic-awesome import
- listhub-wip - auto-generate slug from title (similar mutation pattern)
- listhub-74j - folder visibility

title: GitHub-like repo UX for ListHub
description:
## Vision
Give ListHub the feel of GitHub for browsing and interacting with user repos — without necessarily being GitHub. Users should be able to browse files, see history, clone repos, and download content with the same ease as GitHub.

This is an umbrella for several related features that together create a "GitHub-like" experience natively in ListHub.

## Child tickets
- listhub-j47: Public git repo directory at /git/ (browse repos)
- listhub-2lg: Git history viewer (commits, diffs)
- listhub-ev1: Clone URL + download button
- listhub-2zb: Auto-push to GitHub + encrypted items (complementary — actual GitHub mirroring)

## Key Principle
Even if the underlying storage isn't a traditional filesystem, the UX should feel like browsing a repo. A generated public-facing version of any user's content should be cloneable/downloadable — this could be the actual bare git repo or a dynamically assembled snapshot filtered to public items only.

## GitHub Niceties to Consider
- Green "Code" button with clone URL (git clone listhub.globalbr.ai/@user.git)
- Download as ZIP button
- File browser view (tree of items)
- Commit history with diffs
- README rendering on repo landing page
- Branch/tag support (future)
- Raw file view
- Contributor list

title: Anonymous/unauthenticated editing UX for directory and public lists
description:
## Problem
Currently the "Edit this page" button on the directory only shows for logged-in users. Logged-out visitors see no edit affordance at all. This is a missed opportunity — if someone lands on the directory and wants to contribute, the path to editing should be obvious and frictionless.

## Immediate Fix (do now)
- Show the "Edit this page" button to ALL visitors (logged in or not)
- If not logged in, clicking it should redirect to login/register with a clear message: "Sign in to edit this page" and a link back after auth
- Include inline instructions for AI agents: "Or use the API: POST /api/v1/auth/register to create an account, then PUT /api/v1/directory/:path to edit"

## Brainstorm: Anonymous Editing Options

### Option A: Require authentication (current, with better UX)
- Edit button visible to everyone, redirects to sign-in
- Lowest risk, full audit trail via git author
- Downside: friction for drive-by contributors

### Option B: Shared "anonymous" account
- Single shared account (e.g., "anonymous") that anyone can use
- All anonymous edits attributed to this account
- Simple but no accountability — vandalism risk
- Could rate-limit by IP

### Option C: Per-session anonymous identity
- Generate a temporary anonymous-XXXX username per session
- Edits are attributed to that session identity
- Slightly better accountability than Option B
- Could require solving a captcha before editing

### Option D: Wiki-style anonymous editing (IP-attributed)
- Allow edits without any account, attribute to IP address in git history
- Most frictionless, like Wikipedia for logged-out users
- Git history shows "Anonymous (192.168.x.x)" as author
- Need moderation tools: recent changes feed, rollback, IP blocking

### Option E: "Suggest edit" (PR-like)
- Anyone can propose edits, but they go into a review queue
- Owner/admins approve or reject
- Best balance of openness and quality control
- More complex to build (needs a pending-edits table or branch-per-edit)

## Trade-offs

| Option | Friction | Accountability | Spam Risk | Complexity |
|--------|----------|---------------|-----------|------------|
| A (auth required) | Medium | Full | Low | Low |
| B (shared anon) | Low | None | High | Low |
| C (session identity) | Low | Partial | Medium | Medium |
| D (wiki-style IP) | None | Partial | High | Medium |
| E (suggest edit) | Low | Full | Low | High |

## For AI Agents
Any option works for agents since they can easily register via the API. The real question is whether we want anonymous HUMAN editing. Agents should always use authenticated API calls regardless.

## Recommendation
Start with Option A (better UX for the redirect) as the immediate fix. Then consider Option E (suggest edit / PR-like) as the long-term solution — it balances openness with quality. Option D (wiki-style) is appealing for maximum contribution but needs moderation tooling first.

## Related
- listhub-z0t: Make lists publicly editable
- listhub-xtc: GitHub-like repo UX epic

title: Public-only git mirror — architecture + private block stripping for cloneable wikis
description:
## Goal
Spec and implement a **public-only git mirror per user** so anyone can `git clone` an arbitrary wiki's public portion without touching private items or private content blocks inside otherwise-public items.

## Why this is a separate ticket
listhub-ev1 and listhub-a3e both reference "filtered clone / generated public repo" but stop at the UX layer. This ticket owns the **architecture decision** for how filtering actually happens, because the naive path (point clone at the existing bare repo) leaks private content two different ways.

## What "public" means here — two layers to strip

1. **Whole items** — exclude `visibility IN ('private', 'shared')`. Include `('public', 'public_edit', 'unlisted')`.
2. **Content blocks inside public items** — `<!-- private -->...<!-- /private -->` regions (see `strip_private_blocks()` in `views.py`) must be removed from the markdown emitted to the mirror. These blocks live raw in the DB and raw in the current bare-repo mirror; they're only stripped at Flask render time today, so a `git clone` would expose them.

Plus once listhub-74j ships: **folder-level visibility cascade** — a public item inside a private folder should… what? Excluded? Overriding? Decide and document as part of this spec.

## Current architecture assessment

ListHub already has most of the plumbing, but not wired for public-filtered clone:

| Piece | Status |
|---|---|
| Per-user bare git repos under `bare_repos/<user>.git` | ✓ exists |
| `git_sync.py` writes DB→git incrementally | ✓ exists |
| git-http-backend Smart HTTP | ✓ exists (clone URL works) |
| `strip_private_blocks()` filter | ✓ exists (but only at render time) |
| Visibility filter at mirror level | ✗ missing — all items go into single bare repo |
| Private-block scrubbing at mirror level | ✗ missing — raw content written to git |
| Bare repo exists for every user | ✗ missing — `jacobcole` and likely others have no bare repo (see `~/memory/projects/listhub-strategy-2026-04-08.md`) |

A naive `git clone` today would expose private items **and** private content blocks within public items. Neither existing ticket addresses the second point.

## Approaches considered

### Option A — Second bare repo per user (**recommended**)
- Path: `bare_repos/<username>.public.git` alongside `bare_repos/<username>.git`
- Populated by a parallel `git_sync_public.py` that: (a) filters items by visibility, (b) pre-strips `<!-- private -->` blocks, (c) rewrites YAML frontmatter (`visibility:` kept; anything private omitted), then writes the filtered tree to the public bare repo
- Exposed via existing git-http-backend at a distinct URL, e.g. `https://listhub.globalbr.ai/@<username>.public.git` (or `.git` for public, `-private.git` for auth-required full)
- **Pros**: incremental, preserves git history, anyone can clone without server-side per-request filter cost, composes with existing plumbing
- **Cons**: ~2× git storage per user; public→private transitions leave content in history unless we explicitly rewrite

### Option B — Filter-on-read in git-http-backend
- Single bare repo, smart-HTTP generates packs on-the-fly excluding private content
- **Pros**: one source of truth
- **Cons**: complex (git object rewriting per request), expensive, fragile, hard to cache

### Option C — On-demand export only
- Just ZIP download (listhub-a3e) — no persistent public git mirror
- **Pros**: simplest, always fresh
- **Cons**: no git history, no incremental pull, every clone is a full dump, defeats "clone my wiki and track updates" use case

**Recommendation: Option A.** Storage cost is bearable (markdown is tiny), semantics are clean, composes with existing bare-repo plumbing. Option C is a useful *complement* (listhub-a3e), not a replacement.

## Semantics to decide

1. **Read-only**: the public mirror rejects pushes. One-way export from DB.
2. **History on unpublish**: when an item goes public → private, do we force-rewrite the public mirror's history to scrub it? **Default no** — document that "once published, content may persist in historical commits; removal from HEAD only". Add an admin endpoint to request full scrub if needed. This matches how GitHub treats public→private repo transitions.
3. **Frontmatter**: kept on included items (`visibility: public` stays). Private/shared items are excluded entirely so their frontmatter never appears.
4. **Deleted items**: normal delete commit in public mirror.
5. **Anonymous cloning**: yes, no auth required. Rate limit via existing HTTP rate limiter.
6. **Folder cascade (once listhub-74j ships)**: public item inside private folder → item is excluded (folder visibility wins). Document explicitly.
7. **`.folder.yaml` files (listhub-74j)**: included in public mirror only if the folder is public.

## Dependencies / prereqs

- **Bare repo provisioning bug** — `jacobcole` and probably others have no bare repo at all because signup never calls `git init --bare`. Documented in `~/memory/projects/listhub-strategy-2026-04-08.md`. Needs a sibling ticket + fix before public mirroring matters for those accounts. (File separately.)
- **listhub-74j** — folder-level visibility cascade semantics for filtering (soft dep; can ship without it and revisit)
- **Existing `git_sync.py`** — new `git_sync_public.py` can fork from it

## Enables (downstream)

- **listhub-a3e** — ZIP download shares the filter logic (or just zips up the public mirror directly)
- **listhub-ev1** — clone URL on user profile now points at the public mirror
- **listhub-r62** — agent-native Drive epic: cloneable public data is table stakes
- **LLM-wiki export user story** (`~/memory/insights/llm-wikis-landscape.md`, `~/.claude/projects/-Users-jacobcole-code/memory/llm-wikis-landscape.md`) — the whole point of the Karpathy-style pattern is that agents write flat, humans curate, and the public subset is cloneable as a Karpathy-style LLM wiki. **This ticket is the piece that unlocks "clone Farzapedia's public half".**
- **Archive.org / HuggingFace dataset mirroring** — trivial once the public mirror exists

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `git clone https://listhub.globalbr.ai/@<username>.public.git` works anonymously and returns only public + public_edit + unlisted items
- [ ] `<!-- private -->...<!-- /private -->` blocks are stripped from files in the public mirror
- [ ] Private/shared items never appear in the public mirror (never had a commit, not "removed in HEAD")
- [ ] The owner can still clone the full mirror with auth at a different URL
- [ ] The public mirror auto-updates when items are created / edited / visibility-changed
- [ ] Round-trip verified: `git clone → inspect all files + git log -p → assert no private content leaked`
- [ ] Documented: history retention semantics on public→private transitions
- [ ] Rate limited at the git-http-backend layer

## Related
- listhub-ev1 — parent feature (clone URL + download button)
- listhub-a3e — ZIP download variant (share filter logic)
- listhub-74j — folder-level visibility (affects filter cascade)
- listhub-xtc — GitHub-like repo UX epic (parent)
- listhub-r62 — agent-native Drive replacement epic
- `~/memory/insights/llm-wikis-landscape.md` — LLM wiki user story this unlocks
- `~/memory/projects/listhub-strategy-2026-04-08.md` — bare-repo provisioning bug prereq
- `~/memory/insights/static-site-gen-folder-patterns.md` — physical vs logical hierarchy (affects how the public mirror's folder structure maps to clients like Quartz)

title: Data import epic: bring external lists and directories into ListHub
description:
## Vision
Import existing lists, directories, and curated collections from external sources into ListHub, and build view templates that can render them beautifully — matching or exceeding the original presentation.

## Child Tickets

### Import tasks
- listhub-mlb: Import jacobcole.net lists (91 pages from ~/code/DataExports/)
- listhub-00k: Import flowtools.directory (curated tool directory)
- listhub-tdb: Import Moltbook harvested ideas and knowledge (50K posts, 12 knowledge docs)
- listhub-1hy: Import curated noos knowledge documents

### View/UI features
- listhub-bd1: Directory/showcase view template (like renaissance.engineer, deep-dialogues)
- listhub-ogp: Publishable curated directory UI (like flowtools.directory) — depends on bd1

## Related
- listhub-7aw: Agent-first web epic (imports demonstrate ListHub as a publishing platform)
- listhub-xtc: GitHub-like repo UX (imported content should be browsable/cloneable)

## Why This Matters
ListHub should be the place where all your lists, directories, and curated collections live — not scattered across Google Docs, custom sites, and one-off deployments. By importing existing content AND building view templates, we prove ListHub can replace bespoke sites like renaissance.engineer and flowtools.directory with a single platform.

The Moltbook archive is especially valuable — unique knowledge from an AI agent social network that no longer exists. Publishing it on ListHub makes it permanently accessible.

title: Rebrand to wikihub.md — LLM wiki hosting positioning (Karpathy-style)
description:
## Goal
Launch an upcoming version of ListHub under a new name — **wikihub.md** — repositioned around **LLM wiki hosting** in the Andrej Karpathy sense: flat markdown files, humans-curate / agents-write, cloneable public wikis as the unit of knowledge sharing.

## Why rename
- "ListHub" framing understates what the platform actually is — it's already a multi-user, publicly cloneable, agent-editable, markdown-native wiki host. "List hub" sounds like a Listicle tool.
- **wikihub.md** signals:
  - **.md** → flat markdown files, no proprietary format
  - **wiki** → networked knowledge, not linear docs
  - **hub** → multi-user, cross-linkable, shared discovery surface
- "LLM wiki" is a term Karpathy has used (and others are echoing) for a new category: wikis where LLM agents are first-class writers and humans curate — not traditional wikis, not chat transcripts, not Notion clones.
- It aligns the product with the Karpathy-style LLM wiki user story already surfaced in `~/memory/insights/llm-wikis-landscape.md`.

## What changes

### Identity & positioning
- Primary brand: **wikihub.md** (domain TBD — `wikihub.md` if available, or `wikihub.globalbr.ai` initially)
- Tagline: "LLM wikis for humans and agents" (or similar)
- "ListHub" stays as the current production until the rebrand ships, then redirects.

### Repositioning the home page
- Lead with: "Host your LLM wiki. Clone anyone's. Agents can read and write."
- Feature the Karpathy LLM wiki user story prominently (flat markdown, public clone, YAML frontmatter)
- Highlight `git clone https://wikihub.md/@user.public.git` as a first-class flow

### What stays the same
- All existing data, users, APIs, bare repos, items
- Agent-first primitives (API key auth, REST API, llms.txt)
- Folder/visibility/public-edit system
- Multi-user per-user namespace (`/@username`)

### Naming migration
- **wiki** becomes a first-class term alongside "item" / "list" / "folder" (may replace "list" in most UI copy)
- Top-level user workspace is "your wiki"
- Collections of items under a folder = "sub-wikis"
- Plays nicely with `listhub-a2o` (named wikis) and `listhub-bno` (rename /directory → /featured)

## Dependencies / prereqs

This ticket **depends on** several already-open items being either shipped or nearly so, otherwise the rebrand is wrapping old bones:
- `listhub-xtc.2` — public-only git mirror (the architectural piece that makes "clone anyone's LLM wiki" real)
- `listhub-a3e` — ZIP download (the non-developer version of clone)
- `listhub-bno` — `/directory` → `/featured` rename (frees "wiki" as a term)
- `listhub-a2o` — named wikis (extend /directory model)
- `listhub-9nm` — visibility label consistency
- `listhub-480` — agent onboarding / llms.txt skill (so agents can actually self-sign-up cleanly, which is table stakes for an LLM wiki host)

## Open questions

1. **Domain**: is `wikihub.md` available? Fall-backs: `wikihub.globalbr.ai`, `wiki.md`, `llmwiki.md`, `llmwikis.com`
2. **Rebrand or parallel launch?** Options:
   - (a) Full rename: ListHub → wikihub.md, redirect old URLs
   - (b) Sibling brand: wikihub.md is a new front door pointing at the same backend, ListHub stays as the "legacy" name for existing users
   - (c) Stealth: ship everything under wikihub.md with ListHub as an alias
3. **Copy audit**: how much of the existing UI copy says "list" and needs to become "wiki" / "page" / "note"? (Tied to `listhub-762`.)
4. **Logo / visual identity**: keep current, or rebrand visually?
5. **Agent-first messaging**: is "LLM wiki" the right term, or should we use "agent wiki", "AI wiki", or Karpathy's exact phrasing?

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Decision made on domain (own or stage on subdomain)
- [ ] Decision made on rebrand vs parallel vs stealth
- [ ] Landing page copy drafted and reviewed
- [ ] Migration plan for existing users (if full rebrand): email, banner, URL redirects
- [ ] Prereq tickets shipped or scheduled so the rebrand lands on a coherent product
- [ ] New site live at chosen domain, old domain redirects cleanly
- [ ] llms.txt updated with new naming + clone instructions

## Related
- `listhub-xtc.2` — public-only git mirror (critical prereq)
- `listhub-a3e` — ZIP download
- `listhub-a2o` — named wikis
- `listhub-bno` — /directory → /featured rename
- `listhub-762` — lists vs categories/folders naming
- `listhub-480` — agent onboarding skill
- `listhub-7aw` — agent-first web epic
- `~/memory/insights/llm-wikis-landscape.md` — Karpathy LLM wiki user story
- `~/memory/projects/listhub-strategy-2026-04-08.md` — strategy notes / bare repo provisioning prereq

## Out of scope
- Actual code rename of Python package / repo (`listhub/` → `wikihub/`) — file separately if desired
- Beads prefix rename (`listhub-` → `wikihub-`) — file separately, uses `beads:rename-prefix`

title: Linear-style filtering and UI for roadmap issue tracker
description:
## Goal
Upgrade the /roadmap issue tracker UI to have Linear.app-style filtering, grouping, and display options. Currently it has basic type/status filters and sort options — this ticket would bring it to a full-featured project management view.

## Linear Features to Consider

### Filtering
- **Multi-select filters**: Filter by multiple types simultaneously (e.g., feature + bug)
- **Priority range**: Show P0-P1 only, or P2+
- **Status groups**: Open, In Progress, Blocked, Closed as toggleable groups
- **Assignee filter**: Filter by who's assigned
- **Label/tag filter**: If beads supports labels
- **Date range**: Created after X, updated in last N days
- **Text search**: Filter issues by title/description text
- **Saved filter presets**: "My P1s", "Recent features", etc.

### Grouping
- **Group by**: Type, Priority, Status, Assignee, Epic
- **Collapsible groups**: Each group header shows count, can collapse
- **Nested grouping**: Group by type, then sub-group by priority

### Display Options
- **View modes**: List (current), Board (kanban columns by status), Table (spreadsheet-like)
- **Column visibility**: Toggle which metadata columns show (priority, type, created, updated, assignee)
- **Density**: Compact, comfortable, spacious row heights
- **Description preview**: Show first line of description inline

### Interactions
- **Keyboard navigation**: j/k to move, Enter to open, Escape to close panel
- **Bulk select**: Shift+click to select range, actions on selection
- **Drag-and-drop**: Reorder priority, move between statuses (if writable)
- **URL state**: Filters/sort encoded in URL params so links are shareable (?filter=open&sort=priority&group=type)

### Polish
- **Issue count in filters**: Show (12) next to each filter option
- **Empty states**: "No issues match these filters"
- **Loading skeleton**: Shimmer while fetching from GitHub
- **Responsive**: Collapse filters into a dropdown on mobile

## Implementation Notes
- Currently a single HTML file fetching JSONL from GitHub — could stay that way
- Linear's UI is React + their design system, but we can approximate with vanilla JS
- URL params for shareable filter states would be high value
- Keyboard shortcuts would be a nice touch
- The slide-out detail panel already exists — could enhance with edit capability (if writable via API)

## Phases
1. **Phase 1**: Multi-select filters, text search, URL state, keyboard nav
2. **Phase 2**: Group-by with collapsible headers, view modes (list/board)
3. **Phase 3**: Table view, column config, density options, drag-and-drop

## Reference
- Linear.app UI: https://linear.app
- GitHub Projects board view
- Notion database views (table, board, list, calendar)
